summary_for_rag: Curtis Whitson's flagship 1983 paper. The SPE-12233 method for characterising the heavy end (C7+) of reservoir fluids using a three-parameter gamma distribution, Gauss-Laguerre quadrature for pseudocomponent selection, and Riazi-Daubert correlations with Watson K-factor anchoring. Forty years on, this is the default C7+ characterisation in every commercial reservoir simulator and PVT package. Drew on his 1980 EUR-183 precursor and Haaland's 1981 NTH MS thesis which experimentally widened the alpha range. Sole-author paper, written at age 27 while Curtis was a PhD candidate at NTH.

summary_for_rag: Unpublished 1979 NTH teaching note by Curtis Whitson on the Dykstra-Parsons variability coefficient for layered reservoirs. Gives the closed-form Abramowitz-Stegun rational approximation for the inverse normal CDF, the symmetry trick to cover the full (0,1) range, the log-normal-versus-normal distinction between permeability and porosity, and the complementary-layer relation log K_{100-i} = -log K_i. Written when Curtis was 23 in his first year at NTH after Stanford. An early example of the Whitson pedagogical pattern: name the distribution, give the closed-form shortcut, work an example by hand, point out the symmetry. The same pattern recurs in SPE-12233 (1983) and the 2000 monograph.

summary_for_rag: Unpublished 1979 NTH note by Curtis Whitson extending Knopp's 1965 graphical water-oil relative permeability ratio construction to gas-oil and gas-water systems in a computable three-region equation. The mid-range is log-linear in saturation by least-squares; the two tail regions are exponential deviations from the mid-range, each fit by LSLR on the log-absolute-deviation. The absolute conformance factor V to the plus or minus 1 power gives a dimensionless log-space goodness-of-fit metric. Curtis deliberately omits residual saturations from the correlation, arguing the laboratory cannot measure them reliably. Early example at age 23 of the Whitson methodological signature: fit what is measurable, back-estimate the rest.

summary_for_rag: EUR-183 is the 1980 precursor to Curtis's flagship SPE-12233 C7+ characterisation paper. Presented at the European Offshore Petroleum Conference in London when Curtis was 24, the paper sets out the three-parameter gamma distribution for C7+ molar mass, Riazi-Daubert critical-property correlations with constant Watson K, and a Sturge's-rule-plus-log-spacing regrouping scheme for reducing extended C7+ analyses to a few MCN pseudocomponents. SPE-12233 in 1983 widens the alpha range (using Haaland's 1981 NTH MS thesis results), switches the regrouping to Gauss-Laguerre quadrature, refits the Riazi-Daubert constants for high T_b, and reframes eta as a regression variable. The conceptual scaffolding of every commercial PVT package's C7+ default is set here. Author byline reads University of Trondheim with footnote that Curtis is now at Rogaland District College.

title: Evaluating Constant Volume Depletion Data
journal: Journal of Petroleum Technology
key_findings: Material-balance equations recover liquid compositions, K-values, densities, and C7+ molecular mass from CVD vapour compositions plus liquid saturations with no empirical correlations or EOS

Black-oil PVT properties (B_o, R_so, B_g, R_sg) for volatile oils and gas condensates from CVD-derived phase compositions flashed through separator simulator, extending Dodson-Goodwill-Mayer 1953 without experimental flash separations

Convergence pressure p_K (psia) approximately 60 M_{C7+} - 4200 with slope A(p) = 1 - ((p - p_a)/(p_K - p_a))^n where n is about 0.6
K-values are independent of depletion process - PR EOS simulations of CVD and CCE give essentially identical K-values at the same p, T
Liquid volume errors in S_L amplify into densities and B_o but not into liquid composition - prefer Alani-Kennedy density correlation over material-balance densities
Caution: laboratories that smooth vapour compositions until material balance closes remove the independent QC check
Reducing Watson K_uop of the heaviest C7+ fraction corrects PR EOS liquid-dropout overprediction (NS-1: 32% to 26% versus 22% measured) with little effect on other properties

summary_for_rag: SPE-10067 is the Whitson-Torp 1981 paper that turned the constant-volume-depletion experiment from a set of measured numbers into a working laboratory. Material balance applied to measured vapour compositions and liquid saturations yields liquid compositions, K-values, densities, and the heptanes-plus molecular mass of each phase. Separator-flash simulation of the CVD-derived phase compositions yields B_o, R_so, B_g, and R_sg without the costly liquid-removal flashes of Dodson-Goodwill-Mayer 1953. The Hoffman log Kp vs F plot becomes a data-quality test. The convergence-pressure estimator p_K = 60 M_{C7+} - 4200 and the Watson K_uop adjustment for PR EOS liquid-dropout overprediction enter the practical EOS-tuning toolkit. Presented at the SPE 56th Annual Fall Technical Conference in San Antonio in October 1981. 177 citations. Author byline lists Curtis at Rogaland District College in Stavanger and Torp at NTH. The first SPE paper most reservoir engineers associate with Curtis.

journal: SPE Journal
key_findings: Small differences in C7+ critical properties (few percent in T_c or p_c, 0.05-0.10 in omega) translate into qualitatively different EOS predictions
Same fluid (Eilerts OO-L-544) processed through six critical-property correlations yields six different EOS phase diagrams without any change in measured composition
Different characterisation schemes can change predicted fluid type from gas condensate to volatile oil - the characterisation scheme must be reported alongside the EOS prediction
Omega_a and Omega_b regression is algebraically equivalent to T_c, p_c, omega adjustment with T-tilde_c = (alpha/beta) T_c and p-tilde_c = (alpha/beta^2) p_c
5-10 percent modification guideline: a and b should not be adjusted beyond what corresponds to physically meaningful critical-property changes
Acentric factor matters more in ZJRK than PR because ZJRK uses omega to correct both Omega_a and Omega_b
Practical guidance on Roess Edmister Lydersen Nokay Cavett Hariu-Sage Bergman Kesler-Lee Riazi-Daubert pairings by EOS family and fluid type

summary_for_rag: SPE-11200 is the disciplined-regression companion to Curtis's 1983 SPE-12233 C7+ characterisation paper. Using six reservoir fluids spanning lean gas to black oil and two cubic EOS forms (Peng-Robinson and ZJRK), the paper shows that small differences in C7+ critical properties produce large differences in EOS predictions, that different characterisation schemes can change a fluid from gas condensate to volatile oil without any change in measured composition, and that EOS regression via Omega_a and Omega_b knobs is algebraically equivalent to adjusting T_c, p_c, and omega. The 5-10 percent modification guideline (a and b should not be adjusted beyond what corresponds to physically meaningful critical-property changes) is the closing warning. Originally presented at the SPE Annual Fall Technical Conference in New Orleans in September 1982 and published in SPEJ December 1984. Author byline reads Rogaland Regional College with footnote 'Now with Whitson Oil Inc.' - the first published trace of the consulting practice that becomes Whitson AS in 1988. 80 citations.

summary_for_rag: Curtis Whitson's 1984 SPE-12634 paper proposes using the EOS itself to back-calculate critical properties for petroleum fractions by matching the measured normal boiling point and molar volume at standard conditions, producing internally consistent Tc and pc. Also argues for adjusting the boiling point of the heaviest C7+ fraction before tuning methane binary interaction coefficients to match saturation pressure, on the grounds that Tb is the genuinely uncertain input while BIPs lack physical meaning. Establishes the disciplined property-first BIPs-last EOS tuning hierarchy that becomes the default in commercial PVT packages.

summary_for_rag: Whitson and Sognesand 1986 SPE-15482 paper on rigorous variable-rate well test analysis using van Everdingen-Meyer superposition with the appropriate dimensionless pressure solution rather than the log approximation. Three examples demonstrate how a single linear fit covering drawdown and buildup data simultaneously yields permeability, initial pressure, fracture half-length, mechanical skin, and non-Darcy coefficient. Curtis insists on graphical adjustment over automated regression because the eye catches misfit curvature that regression averages out. A rare side-step from Curtis's PVT work demonstrating the same disciplined rigorous-beats-shortcut pattern.

summary_for_rag: Whitson, da Silva and Soreide 1988 SPE-18315 paper introducing the Generalised Black-Oil (GBO) formulation, a four-component extension of the modified-black-oil model designed for two cases MBO cannot handle: areal or depth-varying compositional variation in volatile-oil reservoirs and non-equilibrium gas injection. Three scalar state variables - r for compositional variation, sigma and tau for injection-gas partitioning - carry most of the relevant physics, with PVT properties blended from pre-computed binary tables. Conceptual precursor to the Singh-Whitson BOPVT series.

title: The Negative Flash
journal: Fluid Phase Equilibria
key_findings: Negative-flash solution is a saddle point of the Gibbs energy surface, not a minimum - the Hessian's ideal part E has one positive and N-1 negative eigenvalues for beta outside [0,1]
Convergence pressure envelope is the boundary where beta tends to plus or minus infinity and K values approach unity
Near the critical point the negative-flash region is at least four times wider than the stability-test region
Wilson K values adequate for most conditions; converged K values from nearby flash are best initialisation
GDEM acceleration is risky for the negative flash; Newton-type methods safe when neighbouring grid cells give initialisation

Continuous derivatives across phase boundaries enable isenthalpic flash, three-phase HC dewpoint, MMP via Jensen-Michelsen 1987, and phase-type indicator (beta less than 0 oil, beta greater than 1 gas) in compositional simulation

Whitson 1983 SPE-12233 C7+ characterisation feeding the EOS
contributed_to: Every commercial flash routine since 1989: Eclipse, CMG, SENSOR, MoReS, tNavigator, Multiflash, REFPROP, whitson code, Python EOS libraries

summary_for_rag: Whitson and Michelsen 1989 paper in Fluid Phase Equilibria 53, 51-71. The foundational negative-flash paper. Provides the thermodynamic proof that the Rachford-Rice flash solution at beta outside [0,1] is a saddle point of the Gibbs energy surface, derives the convergence-pressure envelope as the boundary where beta tends to infinity, and analyses successive-substitution convergence. The construction enables continuous-derivative nested flash algorithms including isenthalpic flash, three-phase hydrocarbon dewpoint with water, minimum-miscibility-pressure calculation, and phase-type indication in compositional reservoir simulation without explicit stability tests. Origin story: Aaron Zick showed Curtis the construction over dinner at an Italian pizzeria in Trondheim in November 1985. Curtis named it the negative flash. At a first DTU meeting in 1988 Michelsen said he failed to see the interest or use. The next morning he rang with 5-6 pages written in green ink on beige paper - the saddle-point interpretation, the convergence proof, and a suggestion to write the paper together with Michelsen as second author. Zick declined first authorship when offered. The paper that resulted set the rigour standard for computational EOS work and started a thirty-year collaboration. Implemented in every commercial flash routine since 1989.

Salinity correction yw(brine)/yw(water) = 1 - 0.02865 csw^1.44 approximately independent of P and T, consistent with Katz et al. 1959 up to 3 M
Dodson-Standing 1944 linear correction overpredicts and is not recommended

summary_for_rag: Soreide and Whitson 1992 paper in Fluid Phase Equilibria 77, 217-240. The foundational EOS-brine framework for petroleum engineering. Extends Peng-Robinson to handle hydrocarbons, CO2, N2, and H2S with pure water and NaCl brine. The CO2/brine correlation has been picked up well outside petroleum engineering in CCS and geothermal applications. Implemented as a built-in option in Multiflash, the whitson code, and most academic EOS Python libraries. The Soreide-Whitson framework remains the standard EOS-brine method in petroleum engineering thirty years on.

title: Application of a General Material Balance for High-Pressure Gas Reservoirs
conference: 66th SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition, Dallas
key_findings: Generalised p/z material balance via cumulative effective compressibility c_e(p) rolls up rock, water, non-net pay, and limited aquifer support
Cumulative compressibility (chord slope from initial) must pair with cumulative pressure drop, not instantaneous c_f with delta-p
Pore collapse is not the primary cause of p/z downward curvature - water-phase support via M ratio is
M = M_NNP + M_AQ accounts for all water volumes in pressure communication with the gas reservoir
Closed-form G/G_x = 1/[1 + 2e-4 p_i^2 c_e] gives quick estimate of linear-extrapolation overestimate
Backcalculation workflow jointly estimates G and c_e(p) from production-pressure history; field examples bracket G within ~5%
builds_on: Hall 1953 and Van Gonten-Choudhary 1969 rock compressibility correlations
Soreide-Whitson 1992 EOS-for-brine framework for c_tw via B_w, R_sw, B_tw
contributed_to: Industry-standard high-pressure gas material-balance workflow
Reserves reassessment for overpressured Gulf Coast and North Sea chalk reservoirs

summary_for_rag: Fetkovich-Reese-Whitson 1991 SPE-22921 generalises the classical p/z gas material balance to high-pressure and overpressured reservoirs using a cumulative effective compressibility c_e(p). Refutes pore collapse as the cause of p/z downward curvature and attributes curvature to water-phase and aquifer support via the M ratio. Curtis at 35, six years into Whitson AS, third author alongside the industry heavyweight Mike Fetkovich. His contribution is the EOS-consistent water-phase compressibility integration drawing on the parallel Soreide-Whitson brine work.

summary_for_rag: Riazi & Whitson 1993 IECR presents a viscosity-based correlation for binary diffusion coefficients in dense fluids that handles both gases (8% AAD) and liquids (15% AAD) across 283 data points. The paper replaces the Sigmund 1976 reduced-density polynomial for liquid systems where it had overpredicted by 80-100%. The work runs in parallel with Curtis's collaboration with Michelsen on the constant-volume diffusion problem driven by the Ekofisk nitrogen EOR programme and the Christoffersen NTH PhD. Second Riazi-Whitson collaboration after the 1983 C7+ paper that used Riazi-Daubert critical-property correlations.

Phase-stability test mandatory after GCE convergence to rule out unstable trivial solutions
Interval-halving GOC search robust against saturated, undersaturated, and near-critical column transitions
Peneloux volume translation does NOT cancel in GCE fugacity - the correction at p^o and p(h) must be retained or gradients are biased
Thermal diffusion with negative k_T for methane can reverse the methane gradient and trigger Rayleigh-Darcy convection when number exceeds ~40

Contains the Gibbs-Duhem shortcut paragraph that came from Curtis's 1992 conversation with Michelsen in Trondheim. Four North Sea fluid examples covering black oil to near-critical oil. The Gibbs-Duhem paragraph is the most-quoted single passage in any Whitson paper; the conversation that produced it is documented in Curtis's 2024 Michelsen recollection.

ECM2 for depleted oil reservoirs: equilibrium-extract gas at current pressure, re-inject incrementally until mixture bubblepoint reaches original bubblepoint
Gas coning during an oil test is the preferred sampling design - reservoir gas flowing through high-oil-saturation pores mobilises retrograde condensate completely

Isokinetic sample heating to 100C does not revaporise carryover; recommend physical volume measurement at separator conditions instead

summary_for_rag: Fevang & Whitson 1994 SPE-28829 is the conceptual pivot of modern gas-condensate sampling. The paper formalises the reservoir-representative vs insitu-representative sample-quality hierarchy and introduces the Equilibrium Contact Mixing (ECM) family. The counterintuitive recommendation that gas coning during an oil test is the preferred sampling design inverts decades of practice. First Fevang-Whitson collaboration; bridges Phase 2 PVT framework to Phase 3 gas-condensate work; supplies the clean-composition input that the 1996 SPE-30714 well-deliverability paper consumes.

title: Modeling Gas-Condensate Well Deliverability
journal: SPE Reservoir Engineering
key_findings: Three-region radial flow model: Region 1 near-wellbore two-phase flow with constant flowing GOR, Region 2 condensate buildup with gas-only flow following CVD curve, Region 3 single-phase gas above dewpoint
Three-part pseudopressure rate equation - integral is geometry-independent; geometry effects enter only through productivity constant C and skin s
Region 1 krg/kro is a direct function of pressure from producing GOR and PVT - saturations never need to be calculated
Region 1 outer boundary p* equals the dewpoint of the producing wellstream - surface measurement defines subsurface flow boundary
krg = f(krg/kro) is THE fundamental relative-permeability relationship for condensate blockage, not krg(Sg) and kro(Sg) separately; relevant range 1 < krg/kro < 50
Critical oil saturation has no direct effect on deliverability - perceived sensitivity is actually sensitivity to the krg = f(krg/kro) curve
Gas-oil IFT has negligible effect on deliverability - threshold IFTs of 0.1-0.3 dyne/cm rarely reached in Region 1 at minimum BHFP
Coarse-grid pseudopressure method eliminates local grid refinement via pre-calculated 3D table lookup
CVD material-balance GOR overestimates blockage loss by ignoring Region 2
Horizontal-well deliverability strongly sensitive to kv/kh - 3 to 10 year plateau swing

summary_for_rag: Fevang & Whitson 1996 SPE-30714 is the landmark gas-condensate well-deliverability paper - the practical industry standard for thirty years. The three-region radial flow model splits near-wellbore flow into Region 1 (two-phase, constant flowing GOR), Region 2 (condensate buildup with gas-only flow following CVD curve), and Region 3 (single-phase gas above dewpoint). The conceptual move is that saturations are not the right near-wellbore variable - producing GOR plus PVT plus krg = f(krg/kro) fully determine the pseudopressure. The 1996 paper is the direct successor to Fevang-Whitson 1994 SPE-28829 sampling paper. 364 OpenAlex citations, the most cited Curtis paper outside the 1983 C7+ work. Implemented in every modern compositional reservoir simulator.

Sampling must be early and at low drawdown; once wells produce at high drawdown the wellstream does not recover

summary_for_rag: The landmark paper that defined the liquid-rich-shale PVT problem. Curtis Whitson and Snjezana Sunjerga show that what LRS wells produce at surface is not what is in the reservoir: producing OGRs run 2-50 times below in-situ values due to ultra-low permeability and large drawdown. The paper introduces the dimensionless producing OGR rpD as oil-recovery efficiency, the multi-mixture CCE laboratory test that replaces CVD for LRS sampling, EOS development from production data when conventional sampling fails, black-oil table extrapolation to the critical point for single-phase initialisation, the 1001-grid requirement for converged simulation, the producing-OGR-versus-FBHP diagnostic for fluid classification, and the rule that sampling must be early and at minimum drawdown. Start of Curtis's Phase 4. Every later unconventional PVT study traces back to this paper.

Universal QC priority: recombination-GOR accuracy buys more phase-behaviour reliability than refined GC protocols

title: Gas Condensate Relative Permeability for Well Calculations
journal: Transport in Porous Media
key_findings: Panfilov monovariance theorem: steady-state two-phase compositional flow has state variance 1 - all thermodynamic variables are functions of pressure alone

title: Modeling Conformance as Dispersion
journal: SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition, Houston
key_findings: Physical dispersivity is a rock property approximately 0.01 ft for consolidated rocks; field apparent dispersivities are 10^4 to 10^5 times larger and reflect conformance not physical mixing

Heterogeneity alone causes no in-situ mixing - the transport equation with alpha = alpha_t = D_0 = 0 is purely hyperbolic, streamline simulation shows concentrations of 0 or 1 only with no intermediate values
Apparent dispersivity decomposes additively: alpha_a approximately alpha + alpha_ap (areal) + alpha_as (stratification)
Scale-dependence of alpha_a with travel distance L is an algebraic artefact of 1-D CD misapplication, not evidence of a physical mechanism
Use physical dispersivity 0.01 ft as input to miscible-flood, tracer-test, bank-breakdown, and chemical-reaction simulations; model conformance explicitly via fine-grid permeability
Cross-simulator validation across Sensor 3DSL and UTCHEM confirms the no-mixing result is physics not implementation

Keith Coats was the author of SENSOR and one of the most influential reservoir-simulation engineers of his generation, making this paper a senior-peer pairing rather than a student-supervisor publication.

title: CO2 IOR Potential in Naturally Fractured Haft Kel Field, Iran
journal: International Petroleum Technology Conference, Doha
key_findings: Haft Kel CO2-oil density inversion at approximately 2050 psia at 110 F; thermodynamic CO2 MMP approximately 1400 psia
Two fundamentally different recovery mechanisms above and below the density crossover - CO2 enters from top below 2050 psia, from bottom above

Matrix-block height insensitivity for CO2 (constant recovery 6 to 14 ft blocks) vs strong sensitivity for HC gas (12.9% at 6 ft to 36.7% at 14 ft)
CO2 dilution by HC gas degrades recovery non-linearly - first 10% dilution costs 40% of OOIP recovery
Ultimate recovery is matrix-height insensitive but time-to-recovery still depends on matrix permeability

title: Miscibility Variation in Compositionally Grading Reservoirs
journal: SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition
key_findings: MMP-depth follows fluid-zone and mechanism archetypes (oil zone monotone, gas-condensate zone equals dewpoint for lean gas, C/V drops below dewpoint for enriched gas)
Zick multicell algorithm with miscibility-distance criterion validated against slimtube within plus-minus 5-15 bar at 20-100x speedup across 14 systems
Critical enrichment E* is largely independent of depth in the oil zone; miscibility depth D* parametrises the two-region MME-depth profile
Miscible cycling can be achieved far below the dewpoint with sufficiently enriched gas (overturns classical assumption)
Enriched-gas slug of around 0.10 PV produces C/V miscibility identical to continuous injection if slug exceeds slug-chase dispersion size
C/V MMP of gas-plus-retrograde-condensate equals C/V MMP of retrograde condensate alone (only liquid composition matters)
Slimtube grid extrapolation does not converge for gas-condensate C/V; multicell algorithms take over

summary_for_rag: Hoier and Whitson 1998 SPE-49269 joins the compositional-grading framework with the Zick condensing-vaporising miscibility mechanism. For each fluid zone (oil, gas condensate) and developed-miscibility mechanism (VGD, C/V) the MMP-depth profile follows a small set of archetypes. The Zick multicell algorithm with miscibility-distance criterion is validated against slimtube simulation. Critical enrichment E* and miscibility depth D* decompose the MME-depth profile. The paper overturns the assumption that gas cycling below the dewpoint cannot be miscible: with sufficiently enriched gas the C/V MMP in the gas-condensate zone drops far below the dewpoint, and a 0.10-PV enriched slug followed by lean chase reproduces continuous-injection performance.

title: Guidelines for Choosing Compositional and Black-Oil Models for Volatile Oil and Gas-Condensate Reservoirs
journal: SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition
key_findings: Stepwise pseudoisation from 22 to 6 components with automated regression at each step preserves accuracy; jumping directly degrades saturation pressure and liquid dropout
Method C initialisation (compute compositional gradient with full EOS, then pseudoise the gradient) gives -0.88% IOIP error vs full EOS
Black-oil initialisation by Rs/rs vs depth gives correct IFIP within 1%; structurally-high wells show 5-10% GOR deviation after 5 years
Method B (depletion of fully-swollen oil) is the best of three BO PVT table extension methods for gas injection
Single-point swelling-test splicing is the recommended procedure for extending BO tables across gas-injection pressure range
BO is always adequate for depletion if Rs/rs initialised properly and tables generated from right experiments; compositional generally recommended for gas injection
Setting rs=0 is NOT a conservative P10 sensitivity when gravity segregation is strong (can over-predict oil recovery)
Near-critical BO data within 30-50 bar of critical point can be deleted to fix simulator convergence without affecting production performance

summary_for_rag: Fevang, Singh and Whitson 2000 SPE-63087 is the industry benchmark for choosing between black-oil and compositional reservoir simulation. The paper builds a generic North Sea reservoir spanning medium-rich gas condensate through near-critical to volatile oil, and runs 22-component EOS, stepwise-pseudoised 6-component EOS, and black-oil through depletion and gas-injection scenarios. Methodological contributions are stepwise pseudoisation with regression at each step, Method C compositional-gradient initialisation for pseudoised EOS models, Rs/rs vs depth initialisation for graded-reservoir BO, three methods for extending BO PVT tables for gas injection (Method B with fully-swollen depletion is best), and the single-point swelling-test splicing procedure. First Singh-Whitson paper - start of a 25-year BOPVT partnership running through to SPE-225382 (2025).

GOC location sensitivity 15 m oil column per bar dewpoint uncertainty for rich condensate, 50 m per bar for lean

Cupiagua field: isothermal gradient from top-structure gas sample overpredicts IOIP by more than factor of 3 (actual field shows near-constant composition)

Management must be warned that only additional samples (delineation wells) can reduce fluid-definition uncertainty

summary_for_rag: Singh and Whitson 2008 SPE-117930 provides the first published verification of the Fevang-Whitson three-region gas-condensate pseudopressure method for layered reservoirs. The coarse-grid model with pseudopressure reproduces the fine-grid radial benchmark across permeability spread of four orders of magnitude, with or without crossflow, with capillary-number modification of relative permeabilities, with non-Darcy beta-flow, and across compositional grading. By-connection (per-layer) pseudopressure is required for gas cycling and selective breakthrough. The paper rebuts Barker IPTC-10382 (2005) by attributing reported failures to simulator implementation rather than method.

The paper argues that high-velocity-flow effects apply to oil as much as gas; Vogel cannot be applied without confirming HVF is negligible. The authors show that the two are identical (the two-point tangent equals material-balance area-equating) and that each applies in a different time regime. Characteristic Whitson unification move: competing methods in the literature are special cases of a regime-based selection framework.

summary_for_rag: Whitson 1989 SPE-20006 is a one-page JPT Discussion of Gold-McCain-Jennings 1989 that pushes back on a multi-correlation, multi-nomograph workflow for retrograde-gas specific gravity. The proposed Leshikar-style simplified workflow uses only primary-separator measurements plus stock-tank oil gravity, leaning on the Watson K characterisation-factor correlation, Standing's bubblepoint, and Katz's gamma_ga correlation. Clean miniature of the Whitson disposition: given the data an engineer actually has, what is the simplest correct calculation?

collaborators.Svein M. Skjaeveland: Rogaland University Centre (now University of Stavanger). Long-time petroleum engineer and capillary-pressure researcher

summary_for_rag: Boe, Skjaeveland & Whitson 1989 SPE-10224 (originally presented 1981 ATCE, submitted Nov 1982, published SPEFE Dec 1989) provides the closed-form S(p) relation for two-phase radial flow in the infinite-acting period that lets the two-phase pseudopressure integral be evaluated from wellbore data alone. Derives a unique dS/dp relation by combining oil and gas diffusivity equations in Boltzmann coordinates. The wellbore integration eliminates absolute permeability so semilog liquid-analogy interpretation can be applied to multiphase drawdown tests. The producing-GOR stabilisation result becomes the theoretical foundation for the Fevang-Whitson 1996 three-region model. Explicit cataloguing of where the framework fails: buildup gives wrong Horner slope, pseudosteady-state breaks Boltzmann transform.

title: A Study of Recovery Mechanisms in a Nitrogen Diffusion Experiment
journal: Unsolicited SPE manuscript (Fina Exploration Norway funded)
key_findings: Coupled analytical-fracture / numerical-matrix compositional model with fully implicit treatment mandatory because pressure gradients are very small while capillary and composition gradients are large

IFT-scaled capillary-pressure correction via Macleod-Sugden parachor and Young-Laplace is critical - without it methane recovery is overpredicted 0.57 vs 0.37 measured at 16 days, and saturation profile becomes monotonic instead of the observed non-monotonic

Phase-saturation correction f_ps = min(S_pj, S_pn) for effective diffusion shuts off transmission when either block has zero phase saturation - arithmetic or harmonic means give unphysical transmission

Each component moves by a different combination of mechanisms: nitrogen by gas-phase diffusion early then gas-phase Darcy, methane by gas-phase diffusion only (Darcy is downward opposing gravity), pentane by upward oil-phase Darcy with downward diffusion compensated by convection

Darcy direction and diffusion direction can be opposite for the same component in the same phase - the four forces (viscous, gravity, capillary, diffusion) must be treated with equal weight in compositional simulators
Switching off intra-core diffusion collapses methane recovery from 38% to 5% at 16 days; pentane recovery only marginally affected because Darcy-dominated
builds_on: Riazi & Whitson 1993 IECR diffusion-coefficient correlations (companion bulk-fluid input)
Whitson-Soreide 1989 capillary-flash work (complementary - VLE insensitive to p_c, flow very sensitive to IFT-varying p_c)
contributed_to: Christoffersen-Whitson 1994 SPE-26673 capillary-pressure work
Operator nitrogen-EOR programmes that need diffusion treated with equal weight to Darcy
Whitson-Brule 2000 SPE Monograph treatment of EOR PVT
Compositional-simulator development where matrix-fracture transfer in chalk-like systems is diffusion-dominated for some components

collaborators.H. Hu: Visiting researcher at NTH on the 1989-1991 nitrogen-diffusion programme
collaborators.Y. Qi: NTH co-investigator on the Ekofisk nitrogen programme

summary_for_rag: Hu, Whitson & Qi 1991 SPE-21893-MS is a 21-page unsolicited manuscript on the IFP nitrogen-diffusion core experiment for Ekofisk. Couples an analytical convection-diffusion solution in the fracture to a 1-D fully implicit compositional simulator in the matrix. Headline result: each component moves by a different combination of mechanisms; for methane, gas-phase Darcy flow is opposite in sign to the diffusive flux because IFT-driven capillary gradients reverse the gas-flow direction relative to gravity. Demonstrates that the four forces (viscous, gravity, capillary, diffusion) must be treated with equal weight. The 1993 Riazi-Whitson IECR paper supplies the input bulk-fluid diffusion coefficients; the 1994 Christoffersen-Whitson SPE-26673 capillary work is the complementary laboratory protocol.

title: C7+ Fraction Characterization (book chapter and 1990 CEC follow-up)
journal: Advances in Thermodynamics, Vol. 1, Taylor & Francis, pp. 35-56; and Chemical Engineering Communications, 96, 259-278 (1990)
key_findings: Modified beta_0 method enables compositionally-related samples to share identical pseudocomponent properties while carrying different mole fractions - prerequisite for thermodynamically consistent gas-cap / oil-column / compositional-grading phase predictions
Empirical alpha-eta correlations reduce gamma fitting from three to two parameters: eta_T = 658[1 - 1/(1 + 27.467/alpha_T^1.659)] and eta_M = 110[1 - 1/(1 + 4.043/alpha_M^0.723)]
Minimum 6 distillation cuts required for stable three-parameter gamma fit; two-parameter alpha-eta fit is preferred fallback for N < 6
Validated on Birba (Oman, oil plus gas condensate with GOR 1500 to 6200 scf/STB) and East Painter (Wyoming, 8 samples with GOR 2500 to 10000 scf/STB) - all saturation pressures matched within 4%
builds_on: ASTM D2892 / D5236 TBP distillation protocols
contributed_to: Hoier-Whitson compositional grading series 1998-2004
Whitson-Belery 1994 SPE-28000 compositional gradients
Younus 2019 URTeC-551 field-wide EOS model development - parent-child architecture rests on identical pseudocomponent properties

Validated on Birba (Oman) and East Painter (Wyoming) fields. The multi-sample anchor is the prerequisite for any field-wide EOS development and for the Hoier-Whitson compositional-grading series.

title: Phase Behavior in Reservoir Simulation
journal: Fourth International Forum on Reservoir Simulation, Salzburg, Austria, 31 August - 4 September 1992
key_findings: Four-tier PVT formulation hierarchy: traditional BO (1x cost), modified BO (1-2x), K-value flash (2-5x), EOS-compositional (10-100x); select cheapest tier that fits the physics, confirm with material-balance comparison against next tier
Traditional BO validity screening: R_s < 750 scf/STB, B_o < 1.4 bbl/STB, API < 30 degrees; definitive test is depletion material-balance comparison against compositional formulation

Six-step stepwise regression pseudoisation: build full 12-20 component characterisation, simulate target multicontact experiments, treat synthetic data as real, apply Coats pseudoisation to preserve EOS constants A and B at initial conditions, fine-tune by restricted regression, repeat for progressively fewer components

Curtis quotes: EOS preferred over MBO for teaching ('faster and more readily understood'); Coats-Smart five-parameter regression as Sledge Hammer / Indy 500 analogy; Peneloux volume translation 'would have made PR and many other cubic EOS unnecessary'
Commercial-simulator implementation bug flagged: assumption gamma_oo = gamma_og fails precisely for volatile reservoir fluids that require MBO
builds_on: Coats 1985 pseudoisation preserving mixture EOS constants A and B
Cook et al 1974 G_i extension for MBO with vaporising gas injection
contributed_to: Whitson-Brule 2000 SPE Monograph Volume 20 - the 1992 tutorial structure becomes the Monograph spine
Fevang-Singh-Whitson 2000 SPE-63087 BO-vs-EOS guidelines
Whitson AS commercial pseudoisation practice
Whitson Academy course content
summary_for_rag: Whitson 1992 HOT Forum is a 56-page solo-authored tutorial at the Fourth International Forum on Reservoir Simulation in Salzburg, eight years before the 2000 Monograph. The tutorial introduces three operational frames the Monograph leaves more implicit: the four-tier PVT formulation hierarchy (BO, MBO, K-value flash, EOS) with cost-vs-fidelity selection rules, the traditional BO validity thresholds with the material-balance validation protocol, and the six-step stepwise regression pseudoisation workflow that treats synthetic multicontact data as the regression target. Source of Curtis's most-quoted analogies including the Sledge Hammer / Indy 500 description of Coats-Smart regression and the Peneloux volume-translation endorsement.

title: Petroleum Reservoir Fluid Properties (AAPG Development Geology Reference Manual)
journal: In Morton-Thompson & Woods (eds.), Development Geology Reference Manual, AAPG Methods in Exploration Series No. 10, pp. 504-507
key_findings: Gas cycling economic-justification thresholds by initial liquid content: onshore OGR > ~50-100 STB/MMSCF, offshore OGR > ~100 STB/MMSCF reflecting larger offshore facility capex
Below the thresholds, pressure depletion with dry-gas sales is typically the better economic outcome; nitrogen injection is a cheaper alternative to lean-gas cycling when N2 is locally available
builds_on: 1990s North Sea and US Gulf Coast operating-cost economics
Curtis's five-category reservoir-fluid classification (here adapted for geologist audience)
contributed_to: Operator screening of gas-condensate fields for cycle-vs-deplete decisions through the 1990s and 2000s
Whitson-Brule 2000 SPE Monograph treatment of gas-cycling economics
Teaching content linking fluid richness to surface-facility scale
summary_for_rag: Whitson 1993 AAPG Development Geology Reference Manual chapter is a four-page pedagogical entry written for a geologist audience. Most content is fluid-property definitions, the five-category fluid classification, and PVT experiment overview - subsumed by the 2000 Monograph. The chapter's unique contribution is one Whitson operational heuristic on when gas cycling is economically justified by initial liquid content (onshore OGR > 50-100 STB/MMSCF, offshore > 100 STB/MMSCF). The onshore-offshore gap reflects offshore facility capex roughly doubling the cycling break-even OGR.

title: Gas-Oil Capillary Pressure of Chalk at Elevated Pressures
journal: SPE-26673-MS (later SPE Formation Evaluation)
key_findings: Thin-membrane apparatus reduces chalk p_c measurement time from months to ~14 days per curve: 6 micron track-etched membrane sandwiched between 105 micron cellulosic membranes, pressure controlled to +/- 5 mbar across 0-1000 mbar

Young-Laplace IFT scaling p_c(sigma) = p_c_ref * sigma/sigma_ref breaks down below ~5 mN/m on Stevns chalk; measured high-pressure p_c is roughly half what linear scaling of low-pressure centrifuge predicts
Two candidate physical mechanisms: apparent contact angle increases as IFT decreases (sigma cos theta scaling), and Cahn-transition phase redistribution in largest pores near critical conditions

Gravity-capillary drainage recovery in fractured chalk is pessimistically biased by linearly-scaled laboratory p_c - the matrix saturation profile retains more oil than the true reservoir; underestimation worst below 2 mN/m
builds_on: Jennings t ~ phi L^2 / k equilibration scaling
contributed_to: Direct high-pressure p_c measurement protocols at Statoil and partner operators 1990s-2000s
Fractured-chalk simulation studies for Ekofisk and analogues
Literature on IFT-scaling breakdown in narrow-pore-distribution rocks
Whitson-Brule 2000 SPE Monograph capillary-pressure section
Singh-Fevang-Whitson 2000 and downstream BO-vs-EOS guidelines

collaborators.Kjell R. Christoffersen: NTH PhD student under Curtis, then Statoil Research Centre. Ran 1989-1991 CVD experiments that motivated Curtis's 1992 Michelsen CVD-code collaboration

summary_for_rag: Christoffersen & Whitson 1994 SPE-26673 measures drainage gas-oil capillary-pressure curves on 5 md Stevns outcrop chalk at 70, 105, and 130 bar (IFT 6.3, 3.2, 1.5 mN/m respectively) using a C1-nC5 binary and a novel thin-membrane apparatus that cuts measurement time from months to 14 days. Key result: Young-Laplace linear IFT scaling of laboratory p_c breaks down below ~5 mN/m; measured high-pressure p_c is roughly half what linear scaling predicts. Engineering consequence: gravity-capillary drainage recovery in fractured chalk is underestimated by linearly-scaled laboratory p_c, with the error worst below 2 mN/m. Part of the Ekofisk PVT trilogy with the 1991 Hu-Whitson-Qi diffusion paper and the 1993 Riazi-Whitson diffusion-coefficient correlations.

title: Evaluating Miscible and Immiscible Gas Injection in the Safah Field, Oman
journal: 13th SPE Symposium on Reservoir Simulation, San Antonio TX, 12-15 February 1995
key_findings: Density-distance hour-glass signature on EOS-slim-tube simulation distinguishes condensing/vaporising from vaporising-only mechanism: CV at MMP shows oil and gas density curves converging to a minimum at the front with two phases on both sides; VM at MMP shows only the left side of the hour-glass with no free gas ahead of the front

Vaporising-only miscibility cannot develop in initially-saturated reservoirs - the mechanism requires single-phase oil ahead of the front; remedying via repressurisation above bubblepoint is a significant capex commitment

Static single-cell multicontact MMP algorithms (Luks 1987, Jensen-Michelsen 1987) systematically overpredict MMP when CV mechanism operates - Safah quantification: MMP_VM = 4525 psig vs MMP_CV = 3310 psig vs experimental 3300 +/- 100 psig (37% overprediction)

Zick 1993 unpublished multicell multicontact algorithm allows simultaneous condensation and vaporisation in successive cells, recovering the CV-mechanism MMP for rich-gas displacements; for lean-gas the static algorithms remain correct
builds_on: Zick 1993 (personal communication) unpublished multicell multicontact algorithm
Luks et al 1987 and Jensen-Michelsen 1987 static single-cell multicontact MMP algorithms (critiqued)
Whitson 1992 HOT Forum pseudoisation (15 to 8 components for Safah)
contributed_to: Whitson-Brule 2000 SPE Monograph Chapter 8 - CV diagnostic and algorithm-overprediction warning become canonical
Alavian-Whitson CO2-EOR series 2005-2010
Operator MMP-design protocols for rich-gas miscible projects with CV mechanism
Commercial MMP software incorporating multicell multicontact methods after Zick's algorithm became available
Pillars of Technology SPE TWA 2009 - CV-mechanism MMP listed among corrections that became standard

collaborators.C. L. Hearn: Operator-side gas-injection engineering on Safah evaluation
collaborators.Aaron A. Zick: Acknowledged for 1993 unpublished multicell multicontact algorithm. Same Aaron Zick from the November 1985 Trondheim pizzeria dinner that introduced Curtis to the negative-flash construction

summary_for_rag: Hearn & Whitson 1995 SPE-29115 operationalises the Zick condensing/vaporising miscibility mechanism for a real reservoir-simulation project at the Safah Field in Oman. The paper introduces three methodological insights: the density-distance hour-glass diagnostic that distinguishes CV from vaporising-only mechanism from one slim-tube simulation, the physical constraint that vaporising-only miscibility cannot develop in initially-saturated reservoirs, and the systematic overprediction (37% at Safah) by published static single-cell multicontact MMP algorithms when CV mechanism operates. The operational reframe: MMP is not a fluid property; it depends on the active mechanism. References Zick's 1993 unpublished multicell algorithm via personal communication to Whitson.

title: Gas Condensate PVT - What's Really Important and Why?
journal: IBC Conference Optimisation of Gas Condensate Fields, London
key_findings: Dewpoint pressure accuracy is usually unimportant for gas-condensate engineering except for GOC prediction via gradient extrapolation

Recoverable condensate by depletion is insensitive to initial compositional gradient in near-saturated reservoirs - rs of equilibrium gas becomes pressure-equilibrated depth-independent below dewpoint
Oil viscosity is the highest-priority PVT uncertainty for accurate condensate-blockage modelling - 2x error in mu_o shifts krg/kro by 2x throughout Region 1
Maximum liquid dropout does not determine condensate blockage severity - what matters is ratio of reservoir pressure loss to pipe pressure loss
Ultimate condensate recovery decomposition for gas-cycling project evaluation: RFo_ult = RF_oD + E_S * RF_oM + (1 - E_S) * RF_oDx (above-dewpoint), with E_S * E_V * RF_oV bonus below dewpoint
Near-wellbore flowing condensate viscosity is 1.5-5x lower than CVD cumulative liquid at same pressure - black-oil tables should be built from CCE on producing wellstream not CVD
Initialise black-oil gas-condensate simulators with Rs and rs versus depth not saturation pressure versus depth to preserve surface volumes in place
Non-monotonic saturated Bo and Rs for medium-lean condensates below dewpoint - first condensate is heavy low-API; two-table workaround recommended
builds_on: Fevang-Whitson 1996 SPE-30714 three-region near-well flow framework
Dr Jeff Creek Chevron ca. 1997 separator-condensate viscosity recommendation
contributed_to: Whitson-Brule 2000 SPE Monograph - the IBC tutorial previews the Monograph gas-condensate chapter
Operator PVT-report specifications prioritising separator-condensate viscosity
Black-oil-table two-fluid initialisation practice at Whitson AS
Singh-Whitson-Nielsen 2025 SPE-225382 BOPVT-vs-lab framework
Nielsen-Wanat-Whitson 2024 SPE-220699 forward compositional uncertainty

collaborators.Oivind Fevang: Co-author of Fevang-Whitson 1996 SPE-30714 and co-recipient of the 1997 SPE Cedric K Fergusson Medal

summary_for_rag: Whitson, Fevang & Yang 1999 IBC is a 28-page tutorial that inverts industry reflex on gas-condensate PVT priorities. Headline provocations: dewpoint accuracy is usually unimportant (except for GOC prediction), recoverable condensate is insensitive to compositional gradient for near-saturated reservoirs, oil viscosity (not maximum liquid dropout) is the highest-priority PVT uncertainty for condensate blockage, near-wellbore flowing condensate viscosity differs by 1.5-5x from CVD cumulative liquid, initialise black-oil simulators with Rs/rs vs depth not p_sat vs depth, and watch for non-monotonic Bo/Rs in medium-lean condensates. The tightest single statement of Curtis's gas-condensate PVT philosophy at the moment the framework crystallised into the 2000 Monograph.

title: Equation of State of a Complex Fluid Column and Prediction of Contacts in the Orocual Field, Venezuela
journal: SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition, Dallas
key_findings: Triple-plot C7+ QC framework for pre-regression sample vetting: Watson Kw vs MW/SG, C7+ molar mass vs p_s, C1 vs p_s; off-trend in multiple plots = remove from regression set

1-D compositional-stability pre-simulation with thermal gradient (200 layers, 25 ft each, artificial phi=1 k=1000 md, 50-75 sim yr) reconciles isothermal EOS gradient with full-field thermal gradient before initialisation

A single 7-pseudocomponent EOS describes a 3000+ ft column across four fault compartments (San Juan 3, 6, 7, 9) ranging from light gas condensate at top to undersaturated black oil at bottom - compartmental difference is depth alignment not fluid system
Untested San Juan 7 GOC predicted at 16,025 ft SS from gradient calculation; ORC-030W drilled April 2000 confirmed GOC at 16,040 ft SS - 15 ft match
Orocual final 7-sample EOS reproduces lab observations within 1-2% across CCE/CVD/DLE/SEP

summary_for_rag: Smith-Bard-Lugo-Yemez-Guerini-Whitson-Fevang 2000 SPE-63088 is the Orocual Field (Venezuela) case study where a 3000+ ft compositionally graded column spanning gas condensate through near-critical to undersaturated black oil is reproduced by a single 7-pseudocomponent EOS across four fault compartments. The paper contributes three Curtis frameworks: (1) triple-plot pre-regression sample QC, (2) 1-D thermal-gradient pre-simulation for proper initialisation, (3) the claim that compartmental differences are usually depth alignment not fluid system. ORC-030W validation: 15 ft GOC prediction accuracy in a previously-untested compartment.

title: Fluid Characterisation for Gas Injection Study using Equilibrium Contact Mixing
journal: 10th Abu Dhabi International Petroleum Exhibition and Conference
key_findings: ECM test as workaround for missing gas-cap sample: recombine separator liquid and vapour ~50/50 by volume, equilibrate 24 hr, measure equilibrium gas and oil phases separately, use as 'representative' GOC data for EOS regression

Multi-sample single-EOS Gaussian quadrature framework: fixed quadrature abscissae, weights, shape parameter alpha; per-sample eta and M_N; all samples share same pseudocomponents with different proportions
Representative-sample selection via trend-match: sample whose gradient prediction best reproduces observed depth cloud of p_s, GOR, C1, C7+ becomes 'most representative' for GOC prediction

Multi-contact swelling (MCV) tests with multiple injection gases essential for near-critical tuning - depletion-only EOS misses MCV saturation pressures by 8-15% at moderate gas ratios, MCV-tuned EOS within +/- 3% across full range
ECM data materially enriches predicted gas-cap C7+ composition: 4.54 mol-% without ECM vs 8.60 mol-% with ECM - fluid classification shifts from lean to medium-rich gas; bias direction is systematic

Five key contributions: ECM as gas-cap-data synthesis tool, multi-sample single-EOS Gaussian quadrature with shared pseudocomponents, trend-match representative-sample selection, multi-contact swelling tests with multiple injection gases for near-critical tuning, and the quantification that ECM data shifts predicted gas-cap C7+ from 4.5% (lean) to 8.6% (medium-rich) - a systematic bias correction that materially changes gas-injection project economics.

title: Implementation of Stream Management at South Natuna Sea Block B, Indonesia
journal: 29th Indonesian Petroleum Association Annual Convention

title: Miscible Gas Injection in Undersaturated Gas-Oil Systems
journal: SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition, Houston
key_findings: Undersaturated gas-oil system: column grades continuously from gas to oil through near-critical transition without saturated GOC; reservoir pressure exceeds saturation pressure at every depth; neighbouring fluid pairs are first-contact miscible

Leading-edge oil bank miscible displacement: once an injection gas develops multicontact miscibility somewhere in the column, the oil bank formed at the front first-contact miscibly displaces any downstream oil regardless of direct injection-gas miscibility

System MMP for undersaturated gas-oil reservoirs equals the C/V MMP at the shallowest depth where condensing/vaporising mechanism develops, not the GOC saturation pressure: Smorbukk South separator gas system MMP 359 bara vs GOC psat 389 bara
CO2-enriched injection gas (15% CO2 added) unlocks C/V at shallower depths and reduces system MMP further to 320 bara at 3795 m for Smorbukk South
Partial pressure maintenance caveat: local depletion below initial saturation pressure flashes downstream oil, breaks the leading-edge oil bank mechanism, and pushes system MMP toward the GOC psat

Point injection at shallowest C/V-MMP depth is preferred IOR strategy - saves ~30 bara operating pressure vs top-crest injection, reduces pressure-maintenance burden, minimises exposure to local psat breach
builds_on: Hoier-Whitson 1998 SPE-49269 MMP variation with depth in compositionally graded reservoirs
Hoier-Whitson 2001 SPE-63085 compositional grading theory and practice
Zick 1986 / 1993 multicell algorithm for CV mechanism
Smith et al 2000 SPE-63088 thermal-gradient pre-simulation
Seto et al SPE-79690 / Jessen et al SPE-84070 streamline simulation

Operator gas-injection IOR programmes in Brent, Smorbukk South, Birba, Cusiana, Bunduq, Orocual
Whitson Academy course material on gas-injection IOR design

summary_for_rag: Hoier, Cheng & Whitson 2004 SPE-90379 extends the Hoier-Whitson 1998/2001 compositional-grading framework to undersaturated gas-oil reservoirs - columns that grade continuously from gas to oil through a near-critical transition without a saturated GOC. The conceptual reframe: MMP is a system property, not a fluid-fluid pair property. The system MMP equals the condensing/vaporising MMP at the shallowest depth where C/V develops, not the GOC saturation pressure. Worked example Smorbukk South: separator gas gives system MMP 359 bara vs GOC psat 389 bara, with CO2 enrichment dropping system MMP to 320 bara. Operational consequence: point injection at the C/V-miscibility depth is preferred over conventional top-crest injection.

title: Khuff Gas Condensate Development
journal: International Petroleum Technology Conference, Doha
key_findings: HKLH (high-permeability low-thickness) zones dominate Khuff well deliverability - each major non-communicating unit (K1, K2, K3, K4) contains ~10 ft HKLH layer carrying 50-90% of total kh while embedded in low-permeability matrix that holds >98% of HCPV; total kh ~5000 md-ft

Constant steady-state blockage skin ~13-14 in well PI calculation reproduces single-well radial-fine-grid plateau ~11 years from coarse-grid 9x9 Cartesian (489-815 ft cells); preferred remedy is true pseudopressure well treatment

Khuff relative-permeability measurement specifications: HKLH core material; krg/kro range 1-50 (most importantly 3-30); low-velocity 1-10 cc/min and high-velocity 50-200 cc/min; plateau uncertainty is order-of-magnitude (7-16 years) across plausible capillary-number exponents

Gas cycling requires multi-HKLH-zone injector/producer pairing and field unitisation (cross-border for North Field/South Pars); 60% ultimate gas recovery, 40% condensate recovery at 2000 psia abandonment in base case
builds_on: Whitson-Fevang-Yang 1999 IBC tutorial on gas-condensate PVT priorities
contributed_to: Operator development plans for North Field, South Pars, Ghawar Khuff through 2010s
Whitson AS commercial Khuff PVT and reservoir-simulation engagements
SCAL specification standard for high-velocity gas-condensate measurements
Singh-Whitson 2008 SPE-117930 layered-reservoir pseudopressure
Whitson Academy course material on gas-condensate well deliverability in layered carbonates

collaborators.A. Kuntadi: NTNU PhD student; later co-author of 2012 SPE-159400 delumping paper with Whitson and Hoda

summary_for_rag: Whitson & Kuntadi 2005 IPTC-10692 is a development-strategy assessment of the Middle East Khuff gas-condensate giants (North Field Qatar, South Pars Iran, Ghawar Saudi Arabia) anchored on four operational insights: HKLH zones dominate Khuff well deliverability (each ~10 ft HKLH layer carries 50-90% of total kh while >98% of HCPV sits in low-k matrix), constant blockage skin ~13-14 as coarse-grid surrogate for pseudopressure well treatment, demanding SCAL specifications for high-velocity relative permeability (plateau uncertainty 7-16 years across plausible nc), and gas cycling requires multi-HKLH-zone architecture plus field unitisation. The two-timescale view: HKLH delivers rate, low-k matrix delivers reserves.

title: Depletion oil recovery for systems with widely varying initial composition
journal: Journal of Petroleum Science and Engineering, Vol 46, 283-297
key_findings: Non-monotonic depletion recovery across GOR sweep 35-25000 Sm3/Sm3 with ~16% minimum at the critical mixture (Rsi ~700 Sm3/Sm3); maximum ~30-40% oil recovery at Rsi ~100 Sm3/Sm3; lean gas condensate recovery rises monotonically to ~92% at Rsi ~25695 Sm3/Sm3

Gas-oil relative permeability sensitivity sharply localised: ~10 percentage point recovery spread (30-40%) at oil GOR ~100 Sm3/Sm3, essentially zero effect on gas-condensate recovery because retrograde liquid stays immobile through depletion

Plateau-rate-constrained optimum GOR shifts up to Rsi ~200-250 Sm3/Sm3 vs ultimate-recovery optimum at Rsi ~100 Sm3/Sm3; plateau oil rate halves between Rsi 100 and 700 Sm3/Sm3; reserves and rate optima are not the same fluid

Coarse-grid plateau-period overprediction ~40% for gas condensates (Rsi 1000-10000 Sm3/Sm3), ~20% for moderate-GOR oils (Rsi 100-200 Sm3/Sm3), negligible for near-critical and volatile oils; arises from numerical dispersion of near-wellbore pressure gradients

Critical gas saturation Sgc controls oil depletion recovery (33-38% spread across Sgc 0-0.20 near GOR optimum) but has zero effect on gas-condensate recovery; SCAL Sgc measurements are recovery-relevant on oils, not on condensates
contributed_to: Singh-Whitson 2009 SPE-120743 full-pressure-maintenance gas-injection counterpart - critical-mixture recovery flips from worst to best
Operator development-screening practice for compositionally graded reservoirs
SCAL budgeting guidance distinguishing reserves measurements from deliverability measurements
Whitson-Brule 2000 Monograph fluid-type recovery treatment (the JPSE paper expands the qualitative argument with numbers)

collaborators.Kameshwar Singh: Whitson AS senior engineer, long-running black-oil PVT collaborator since 2000 SPE-63087
collaborators.Oivind Fevang: PERA. Fevang-Whitson partnership through gas-condensate and systematic fluid-spectrum work

summary_for_rag: Singh, Fevang & Whitson 2005 JPSE maps depletion recovery across a continuous GOR sweep from 35 to 25000 Sm3/Sm3, generated by recombining a single critical mixture to different gas-oil ratios. Headline result: non-monotonic recovery with a 16% minimum at the critical mixture, rising to 30-40% oil recovery at Rsi ~100 Sm3/Sm3 and 92% gas recovery at Rsi 25695 Sm3/Sm3. Three companion sensitivities: relative-permeability uncertainty is sharply localised on the moderate-GOR oil side (zero on gas condensates), plateau-rate optimum shifts up from the recovery optimum (reserves and rate are not the same fluid), and coarse-grid plateau-period error is 40% for gas condensates without pseudopressure treatment.

title: Understanding Gas-Condensate Reservoirs (Oilfield Review)
journal: Oilfield Review (Schlumberger), Winter 2005/2006, pp. 14-27
key_findings: Condensate blockage approximately doubles near-well reservoir pressure drop once BHFP drops below dewpoint and near-well condensate saturation exceeds critical for flow; importance depends on ratio of reservoir to total system pressure drop (low-kh suffers, high-kh resilient); field examples Arun >50%, Oman 67%, Exxon complete well death

Positive coupling (capillary number Nvc effect) and Forchheimer inertial effects compete at high near-well velocities; positive coupling raises krg above low-rate value when IFT is low, Forchheimer reduces apparent permeability at highest velocities; net effect usually positive, requiring laboratory coreflood at both low (1-10 cc/min) and high (50-200 cc/min) rates

Lean gas condensates suffer comparable normalised productivity loss to rich ones - common field-development misconception; steady-state Region 1 saturation is controlled by krg/kro and flowing CGR not by total liquid dropout volume; lean condensates have smaller Region 1 but high enough condensate saturation within that region
builds_on: Fevang-Whitson 1996 SPE-30714 three-region near-well flow framework
Henderson et al 2000 laboratory coupling and Forchheimer measurements
Field datasets: Arun (Indonesia), Oman fields (Shell/PDO), Exxon cases
contributed_to: Operator gas-condensate development screening and blockage-assessment workflows through late 2000s-2010s
Whitson-Kuntadi 2005 IPTC-10692 Khuff paper - kh-ratio framework becomes the HKLH physics
Singh-Whitson 2008 SPE-117930 layered pseudopressure paper
Whitson Academy course material on gas-condensate well deliverability
summary_for_rag: Fan, Harris, Jamaluddin, Kamath, Mott, Pope, Shandrygin & Whitson 2005 is the Schlumberger Oilfield Review (Winter 2005/2006) review of gas-condensate engineering. Three Whitson-anchored arguments anchor the article: condensate blockage doubles near-well reservoir pressure drop (severity depends on kh), positive coupling and Forchheimer effects compete at high velocities (net usually positive but lab-measurement-dependent), and lean gas condensates suffer comparable normalised productivity loss to rich ones (the steady-state Region 1 saturation is set by krg/kro not by total liquid dropout volume). The most widely-read venue available for the lean-condensate caveat that had been in SPE journals since 1996.

title: Pressure/Volume/Temperature (PVT) Treatment (SPE Petroleum Engineering Handbook, Section 17.7)
journal: In Lake (ed) and Holstein (vol ed), Petroleum Engineering Handbook Vol V, SPE Richardson TX, pp. V-1449 to V-1457
keywords: phase stability Michelsen vs saturation pressure
key_findings: Phase-type determination in EOS simulators trades off Michelsen stability test (slow but reliable near-critical) vs saturation-pressure calculation (fast with good K-value start but may fail or give trivial solution near-critical); near-critical compositionally graded reservoirs favour stability test, depletion-dominated oils favour saturation-pressure shortcut with stability fallback

Density errors amplify ~5x into gravity-segregation potential errors - +/-5% phase density errors give ~25% segregation-driving-force error because segregation is driven by density differences not absolute densities; relperm error scales as krn for Corey exponent n=2-4 so 5% density error gives ~15% kr error

Three-phase L1-L2-V flip-flop in two-phase-only EOS simulators causes timestep cuts, pressure oscillations, and failed timesteps - L2 light liquid appears below ~140 F with CO2- or NGL-enriched gas injection; remedies include three-phase-capable simulator, EOS re-tuning to discourage L2 (incorrect thermodynamics but stable numerics), or constant blockage-skin acceptance of the timestep-cut cost
contributed_to: SPE Handbook - one of the most-distributed PVT-in-simulation references in the field
Graduate petroleum-engineering simulation courses worldwide
Operator simulation-protocol documents citing the handbook section directly
2009 SPE TWA 'Pillars of Technology' piece
CO2-EOR three-phase-capable simulator adoption argument through 2010s
summary_for_rag: Whitson 2006 SPE Petroleum Engineering Handbook Section 17.7 is a 12-page Whitson-authored section inside the Holstein-edited reservoir-simulation chapter co-authored with Batycky, Thiele, Coats, Grindheim, Ponting, Killough, Settari, Thomas, Wallis, Watts. Three operational insights unique to the handbook: the Michelsen-stability vs saturation-pressure phase-type tradeoff, the ~5x amplification of phase-density errors into gravity-segregation potential errors, and the three-phase L1-L2-V flip-flop pathology in two-phase-only EOS simulators (CO2 and NGL-enriched gas injection below 140 F). Canonical PVT-in-simulation reference reaching well beyond the SPE-journal community.

title: Analytical Treatment of a Push-Pull Echo Test
journal: Transport in Porous Media, Vol 77, 399-415
key_findings: Semi-analytical streamline model for single-well push-pull echo tests superimposes a radial source/sink on a uniform natural-drift velocity per layer; production-phase streamlines connected to injection-phase streamlines via closed-form stream function; zero physical dispersivity, no numerical dispersion

Natural drift alone produces linear scale-dependency of apparent dispersivity on log-log plot with slope ~1, matching empirical trend from Arya 1988 and Mahadevan 2002 literature compilations without invoking any physical dispersion mechanism - drift distorts injectant area from circle to downstream-skewed shape, smeared production profile from geometry not mixing

Pickens-Grisak SW1 and SW2 single-well tests in stratified Canadian aquifer (b=8.2 m, phi=0.38, K~14.8 darcy, drift 0.17 m/day) reproduced by streamline model without parameter fitting using only reported layer conductivities and head gradient; multi-layer model reproduces SW2 oscillatory shoulder features that single-layer cannot - the upstream high-k breakthrough plus downstream drift-carried arrivals signature
builds_on: Coats-Whitson-Thomas 2004 SPE-90390 apparent-vs-physical dispersivity for multi-well floods (the precursor argument)
Bear 1988a analytical injection-phase solution for radial source/sink
Arya 1988 / Mahadevan 2002 literature compilations of apparent dispersivity vs travel length
Perkins-Johnston 1963 reference physical dispersivity ~0.001 ft unconsolidated sandpacks
contributed_to: Groundwater-hydrology and CO2-storage tracer-test interpretation - deterministic alternative to fitted dispersion models
Reservoir-engineering simulation-input recommendations: physical dispersivity ~0.01 ft not apparent fitted values
Single-well chemical tracer test analysis for residual-oil-saturation determination in IOR
Operator tracer-test interpretation workflows in stratified reservoirs
Follow-up 2010 Johnsen-Whitson TIPM paper extending the framework

collaborators.Sverre Gullikstad Johnsen: NTNU PhD graduate 2007 with Curtis as co-supervisor; two TIPM papers with Curtis; subsequent career at SINTEF on computational fluid dynamics and porous-media flow

summary_for_rag: Johnsen & Whitson 2008 TIPM extends the Coats-Whitson-Thomas 2004 SPE-90390 argument (apparent dispersivity is a conformance artefact, not a rock property) from multi-well pattern flood geometry to single-well push-pull echo-test geometry. Semi-analytical streamline model with zero physical dispersivity reproduces the Pickens-Grisak 1981 SW1 and SW2 effluent profiles - including the SW2 oscillatory shoulder features - without parameter fitting, using only reported layer conductivities and regional groundwater drift. Natural drift alone produces linear scale-dependency of apparent dispersivity matching the literature compilations. Refutes the long-standing groundwater-hydrology orthodoxy that aquifer dispersivity is a scale-dependent rock property.

title: Gas Injection IOR for Widely Varying Initial Compositions
journal: SPE EUROPEC/EAGE Annual Conference and Exhibition, Amsterdam
key_findings: Gas-injection recovery rises monotonically with GOR under full pressure maintenance: 34% at GOR 22, 48% at GOR 300, 75% plateau at GOR 500-6000, 88% at GOR 25000 Sm3/Sm3 - converts the 16% near-critical depletion minimum into a 75% gas-injection plateau

Permeability arrangement asymmetric: high-k-at-bottom gives 67% vs 52% high-k-at-top recovery at GOR 625; same Dykstra-Parsons 0.75; the *arrangement* matters as much as the magnitude of heterogeneity in dipping reservoirs

Methane vs lean-gas crossover near GOR 1000 Sm3/Sm3: oil systems favour lean gas (+2-4 pts via C3-6 swelling and vaporization), gas systems favour pure methane (+2-6 pts via larger chemical-potential gradient for re-vaporising condensate)
Critical-fluid IOR opportunity is 60 percentage points (16% depletion vs 75% gas injection) - the single largest IOR opportunity available from fluid composition alone, independent of rock properties
builds_on: Zick condensing/vaporising mechanism for rich-gas injection
contributed_to: Operator IOR screening for compositionally graded reservoirs spanning the critical mixture
Methane vs lean-gas-cycling design guidance for gas condensates
Permeability-arrangement screening as a first-order IOR variable
Whitson Academy course material on gas-injection IOR design across the fluid taxonomy

collaborators.Kameshwar Singh: PERA. Long-running BOPVT and gas-injection collaborator since 2000

summary_for_rag: Singh & Whitson 2009 SPE-120743 is the gas-injection IOR companion to the 2005 JPSE depletion paper. Same fluid suite (GOR 22-25000 Sm3/Sm3) under full pressure maintenance lean-gas injection in a 99-layer dipping reservoir. Headline result: gas injection produces a monotonic S-curve with 75% plateau across the near-critical region - converting the 16% depletion-minimum critical mixture into the best-performing IOR fluid type. Secondary findings: permeability arrangement (high-k-bottom vs top) is 15-30 point asymmetric for oil-dominated fluids; methane vs lean-gas optimum crosses over near GOR 1000 Sm3/Sm3.

title: Model-Based Integration and Optimization - Gas Cycling Benchmark
journal: SPE EUROPEC/EAGE Annual Conference and Exhibition, Amsterdam
key_findings: First open IAM benchmark: two SPE-3-derived gas-condensate reservoirs + 4-stage separator + dew-point controller (DPC) + NPV with price escalation, discount, CO2 penalty, DPC capital cost; 4 optimization variables; ~10% NPV uplift over base case

Coupling error scales with project time step delta_tP and inversely with sub-divisions Ns per step; recommended operating point delta_tP=1000 days Ns=100 gives <0.5% NPV error; vendor IAM claims should specify both

IAM NPV objectives are multi-modal (DPC temperature has two local maxima at the bounds with interior minimum) and often degenerate along lines (constant maximum across (fg, fgl) combinations); 1-D parameter analysis essential before automated optimization
builds_on: Suryanto-Ekstrand-Whitson-Uleberg-Hoda 2003 IPA03 BOz and z-to-z conversion
Bailey et al 2005 / Cullick et al 2003 IAM case studies (motivating the open-benchmark argument)
contributed_to: Rahmawati-Whitson-Foss-Kuntadi 2010 SPE-130768 three-reservoir extension
Juell-Whitson 2013 SPE-166380 LRS NPV optimisation
Ghasemi-Whitson 2014 IPTC-18033 thermal-SAGD optimisation
Petrostreamz Pipe-It commercial product (sold to Tieto 2018)
Operator IAM workflows for gas-cycling field-development design

collaborators.Aleksander Oma Juell: NTNU PhD student under Curtis; gas-cycling benchmarks; later co-author 2013 LRS NPV paper
collaborators.Mohammad Faizul Hoda: PERA. Earlier co-author of 2003 IPA03 Belanak stream-management paper

summary_for_rag: Juell, Whitson & Hoda 2009 SPE-121252 introduces the first open IAM benchmark for gas-cycling project optimization. Two SPE-3-derived gas-condensate reservoirs + 4-stage separator + dew-point controller + NPV. Three contributions: the benchmark itself, coupling-error characterisation (delta_tP=1000d, Ns=100 gives <0.5% NPV error), and the demonstration that IAM NPV surfaces are multi-modal and often degenerate - 1-D parameter analysis essential. The first public-domain demonstration of Curtis's Pipe-It / Petrostreamz integration stack (Petrostreamz founded 2006, sold to Tieto 2018).

title: Modeling CO2 Injection in a Fractured Chalk Experiment
journal: 2009 SPE/EAGE Reservoir Characterization and Simulation Conference, Abu Dhabi
key_findings: Capillary-threshold-height diagnostic (h_c = P_c,e/(rho g)) distinguishes gravity-drainage from viscous regimes; Karimaie Faxe-chalk h_c=2.7 cm but front propagates below it, so experiment is viscous-dominated not gravity-drainage-dominated as claimed by experimentalists (Wood's-metal residue left fracture k=26 md not 100 D)

Saturation-based recovery factor RF = 1 - S_o_bar/S_o_ini from simulation avoids matrix-fracture-partition ambiguity in cumulative produced oil; report both produced-oil RF and saturation-based RF alongside model match

Separator temperature is first-order uncertainty source; CO2 has lower thermal conductivity than C1-nC7 so gas stream cools less efficiently; Karimaie regressed T_sep 5.8 C (equil gas) vs 26.7-30 C (CO2) - 24 C step-change explains step-change in oil production

Molecular diffusion negligible in viscous-dominated fractured-core CO2 experiments (advective flux sweeps compositional gradients before diffusion acts); confirms Hoteit-Firoozabadi 2006 in different geometry
builds_on: Karimaie 2007 PhD and Karimaie-Torsaeter 2008 SPE-113601 (the laboratory experiment being reinterpreted)
Christoffersen-Whitson 1994 SPE-26673 gas-oil capillary pressure in chalk
Alavian-Whitson 2005 IPTC-10641 Haft Kel field-scale CO2 framework
Da Silva-Belery 1989 extended Sigmund diffusion correlation
contributed_to: Alavian-Whitson 2010 SPE-129666 scale-dependence-of-diffusion paper (gravity-drainage companion)
Alavian-Whitson 2010 laboratory-experiment-design protocol
Operator CO2-EOR programmes in fractured chalk requiring regime classification
Statoil and partner research-institute laboratory-design practice through 2010s

collaborators.Sayyed Ahmad Alavian: NTNU PhD student under Curtis. Four-paper CO2-EOR series with Curtis

summary_for_rag: Alavian & Whitson 2009 SPE-125362 is a model-based reinterpretation of the Karimaie 2007 fractured-chalk CO2-injection experiment. The headline finding overturns the experimentalists' regime classification: the experiment is viscous-dominated, not gravity-drainage-dominated, because the Wood's-metal fracture-sealing technique left residual sealant after re-opening (fracture porosity ~93%, permeability ~26 md instead of ~100 D). Three transferable methodological contributions: the capillary-threshold-height diagnostic for regime classification, the saturation-based recovery factor as an alternative metric when matrix-fracture partition is ambiguous, and the separator-temperature uncertainty caveat for history matching.

title: Scale Dependence of Diffusion in Naturally Fractured Reservoirs for CO2 Injection
journal: 2010 SPE Improved Oil Recovery Symposium, Tulsa
key_findings: Diffusion dominates CO2 recovery RATE in gravity-capillary-drainage fractured reservoirs - opposite to viscous-dominated regime of SPE-125362; lab 0.2x0.2x2 ft block: 7% vs 100% recovery without vs with diffusion at 1000 psia; 8-ft Haft Kel cube: 22% vs 78% at 1000 psia, >90% at 1500 psia in 2-10 years
Whether diffusion matters is a REGIME question not universal: use capillary-threshold-height diagnostic (SPE-125362) to classify regime then include/exclude diffusion accordingly

CO2 fracture-replenishment rate controls vaporisation-gas-diffusion mechanism: 16x rate drop (lab 0.5 to 0.03 cc/min) degrades recovery from 100% to 15% in 10 days; 100x rate drop (field 3 to 0.03 Mscf/D) degrades 10-year recovery from 78% to 62%; below threshold rate fracture becomes CO2-depleted

Matrix-block dimension scales recovery RATE inversely while ultimate recovery converges above MMP: 8, 16, 35 ft cubes all reach ~90% at 1500 psia, only timescale differs; below MMP block-size affects ultimate too
Lab-to-field warning: diffusion path length grows faster than advective replenishment; lab-scale days do not extrapolate to field-scale years

Lab experiment design protocol: 5-25 in2 horizontal x 10-30 in tall reservoir/outcrop sample, MMP_1D-matched binary model oil, 100 D fracture, pressure bracket above and below MMP, multiple injection rates
builds_on: Alavian-Whitson 2005 IPTC-10641 Haft Kel field-scale framework
contributed_to: Operator CO2-EOR programmes in naturally-fractured carbonates (Haft Kel, Ekofisk analogues)
Lab-design standards for fractured-reservoir CO2-EOR experiments (MMP-matched binary protocol)
Ghasemi-Whitson 2014 thermal-SAGD work where diffusion-regime arguments carry over
SPE Monograph treatment of fractured-reservoir CO2 recovery

collaborators.Sayyed Ahmad Alavian: NTNU PhD student under Curtis. Third of the four-paper CO2-EOR series

summary_for_rag: Alavian & Whitson 2010 SPE-129666 quantifies the molecular-diffusion contribution to CO2 recovery in gravity-capillary-drainage fractured reservoirs at both lab and field scale. Headline result: diffusion dominates recovery RATE (opposite to viscous-dominated regime). The 2009-2010 Alavian-Whitson papers together establish the diffusion-regime classification framework: whether diffusion matters depends on regime via the capillary-threshold-height diagnostic. Additional findings: CO2 fracture-replenishment rate controls the vaporisation mechanism, matrix-block dimension scales rate inversely but ultimate recovery converges above MMP, lab-to-field extrapolation is non-trivial because diffusion path length grows faster than advective replenishment.

title: Multi-Field Asset Integrated Optimization Benchmark
journal: SPE EUROPEC/EAGE Annual Conference, Barcelona
key_findings: Three-reservoir IAM benchmark extends Juell-Whitson-Hoda 2009: two gas-condensate reservoirs + one oil reservoir with miscible WAG injection (SPE-5 derived) + full HYSYS surface facility (CO2 removal, NGL plant with DPC, three-stage liquid + two-stage gas separation, gas reinjection); 5-D optimization gives 15% NPV uplift over base case (vs 9% for surface-only scenario)

Simultaneous WAG (shortest cycle) gives highest NPV for miscible WAG oil reservoirs: NPV monotonically decreases with WAG cycle length over 30-365 days range; optimised cycle converges to 42.5 days (operationally limited by surface-facility switching, not reservoir physics)

HYSYS expects NEGATIVE Peneloux volume-shift parameter input - sign convention inverted vs reservoir simulators and PVT packages; 5-10% liquid density error if missed, compounds into NGL/condensate volume errors and NPV errors; verify by cross-checking liquid density at separator conditions against PhazeComp
builds_on: Killough-Kossack 1987 SPE-5 Comparative Solution Project (oil reservoir base case)
contributed_to: Petrostreamz Pipe-It commercial product development
Operator IAM workflows for multi-field complexes with gas cycling + WAG + NGL extraction
Ghasemi-Whitson 2014 IPTC-18033 thermal-SAGD IAM optimisation
Academic comparisons of integrated-optimization algorithms on a shared benchmark

collaborators.Silvya Dewi Rahmawati: NTNU PhD student under Curtis and Bjarne Foss on WAG and integrated optimization
collaborators.Bjarne Foss: NTNU Engineering Cybernetics Department; optimization expertise complementing Curtis's reservoir engineering on IAM
collaborators.Arif Kuntadi: Co-author also on 2005 IPTC-10692 Khuff paper and 2012 SPE-159400 delumping paper

summary_for_rag: Rahmawati, Whitson, Foss & Kuntadi 2010 SPE-130768 extends the Juell-Whitson-Hoda 2009 IAM benchmark from two gas-condensate reservoirs to three reservoirs (adding a SPE-5-derived oil reservoir with miscible WAG) and from simple process to full HYSYS surface facility (CO2 removal, NGL plant, multi-stage separation, gas reinjection). Three contributions: the multi-field IAM benchmark with full surface facility, the finding that simultaneous WAG (~42 day cycle) gives highest NPV for miscible-WAG oil reservoirs, and the HYSYS-volume-shift-sign caveat for multi-vendor IAM stacks.

title: Advanced Fluid Characterization of Pauto Complex, Colombia
journal: SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition, Florence
key_findings: Systematic PVT-data QC workflow with three independent checks: Z-factor via Hall-Yarborough+Wichert-Aziz (excludes outliers >5% deviation), forward material balance, backward material balance; Pauto example excluded one of three labs' Z-factors at high pressure

Non-exponential C7-C12 multiplier extension of gamma C7+: x_i = m_i * x_i_exp with sample-independent m_i (Pauto: C7=0.70, C8=1.29, C9=1.13, C11=0.74, C12=0.71) captures source-rock fingerprint while keeping gamma exponential tail for C13+

Pivot-shift Tc regression formula: Tc_tuned = f_Tc * (Tc_initial - 950 R) + 950 R preserves K-value monotonicity by scaling spread around the average C7+ Tc (~528 K); direct scaling Tc_tuned = f * Tc_initial distorts the spread

K-value consistency check is mandatory after extensive BIP regression: non-monotonic BIPs with opposite signs (Pauto: BIP C1-C5-6 +0.068, BIP C1-C7-10 -0.179) can produce non-physical K-values; verify K_i monotonically decreases with MW and K_i(p) curves do not cross
contributed_to: Whitson AS field-wide EOS development practice through 2010s and 2020s
Younus-Whitson 2019 URTeC-551 field-wide EOS workflow
Younus-Whitson-Martinsen 2022 SPE-211391 GC quality-control framework (2010 QC workflow extended with gamma-model formalism)
