Nielsen-Wanat-Whitson 2024 SPE-220699 forward compositional uncertainty
Operator EOS-tuning practice at BP Exploration Colombia, ECOPETROL, and partners on Pauto Complex

collaborators.Sissel Ø. Martinsen: PERA / Whitson AS. Long-time Whitson colleague on field-wide EOS development

summary_for_rag: Martinsen, Castiblanco, Osorio & Whitson 2010 SPE-135085 is a case-history EOS characterisation of the Pauto Field Complex in the Andean Foothills basin, Colombia: 8 compartmentalised gas-condensate and volatile-oil reservoirs, single 12-component PR79 EOS fit to 11 samples with multi-laboratory PVT data including identifiably erroneous Z-factors. Four methodological contributions: systematic three-check PVT-data QC workflow (Hall-Yarborough Z plus forward/backward material balance), non-exponential C7-C12 multiplier extension of gamma C7+, pivot-shift Tc regression formula preserving K-value monotonicity, and the K-value consistency check as mandatory QC after extensive BIP regression.

title: On the Loss of Injectant and Apparent Dispersivity in Single-Well Push-Pull Tests Influenced by Natural Drift
journal: Transport in Porous Media (Open Access)
key_findings: Closed-form critical injection-phase duration Tc beyond which injectant is lost to drift: Tc = -[Q_p + U_u r_w - Q_i ln|(U_u r_w + Q_i)/(Q_i - Q_p)|]/U_u^2; closes the implicit recoverability assumption of the 2008 streamline model

Single-layer apparent-dispersivity correlation from Monte-Carlo regression of 1000 streamline runs: N_Pe = 7.5 (T_i/T_c)^-1 (Q_i/Q_p)^0.6 +/- 30%, equivalently alpha_a = 0.13 L (T_i/T_c) (Q_i/Q_p)^-0.6; reproduces empirical linear log-log scale-dependency without invoking physical dispersion

Stratified push-pull production-profile prediction via Muskat 1949 permeability-thickness weighting of single-layer profiles reproduces Pickens-Grisak SW1 and SW2 without parameter fitting; +/-30% N_Pe bracket falls within field-data scatter
builds_on: Johnsen-Whitson 2008 TIPM 77, 399-415 (the streamline model being correlated)
contributed_to: Routine practitioner-level tracer-test interpretation in stratified aquifers and reservoirs
Reservoir-engineering simulation-input recommendations: physical dispersivity (~0.01 ft) not apparent fitted values
Single-well chemical tracer test analysis for residual-oil-saturation determination
CO2-storage tracer-test interpretation in stratified geological storage formations

collaborators.Sverre Gullikstad Johnsen: NTNU PhD 2007 co-supervised by Curtis; two TIPM push-pull papers; subsequent career at SINTEF on CFD and porous-media flow

summary_for_rag: Johnsen & Whitson 2010 TIPM (Open Access) is a 4-page follow-up to the 2008 Johnsen-Whitson push-pull paper that derives three analytical results: closed-form critical injection-phase duration Tc, a Monte-Carlo single-layer Peclet-number correlation N_Pe = 7.5 (T_i/T_c)^-1 (Q_i/Q_p)^0.6, and a layer-by-layer Muskat 1949 summation that predicts the Pickens-Grisak SW1/SW2 stratified-aquifer profiles from the single-layer correlation. The closed-form stack replaces the 2008 paper's full streamline integration with a practitioner-level tool to interrogate any echo-test apparent dispersivity. Reinforces the 2008 conclusion that macro-scale apparent dispersivities are a conformance fingerprint of drift plus stratification, not a scale-dependent rock property.

title: Reservoir Fluid Characterization and Application for Simulation Study (OMV Tunisia)
journal: SPE EUROPEC/EAGE Annual Conference and Exhibition, Vienna
key_findings: MDT oil-based-mud contamination cleanup via iterative gamma-exponential fit: subtract mud, refit C7+ to alpha=1 exponential, regress on contamination fraction w_c; Tunisia cleaned six samples at 16-78 wt% STO contamination
Zone and missing-layer fluid-composition reconstruction from clean-up-test OGR via STO/STG recombination regression with Muskat 1949 mobility-weighted layer mixing

LBC oil-viscosity tuning recipe: per-SCN Zc anchored to Orrick-Erbar liquid-viscosity at T_res and 1 atm (shape fit), then single common C7+ Zc multiplier against reservoir-fluid viscosity (level fit) - separates over-parameterised direct fit
Single 34-component EOS pseudoised to 15 components represents four zones (near-critical oil to lean gas condensate) across nine non-communicating layers from a single well
builds_on: Singh-Fevang-Whitson 2007 SPE-109596 BOPVT + LBC component-specific tuning
contributed_to: Whitson AS commercial OBM-decontamination practice through 2010s
Operator MDT sampling and EOS-tuning workflows where contamination is the norm
Nielsen-Younus 2025 SPE-225470 end-to-end fluid-model-development workflow
Younus-Whitson-Martinsen 2022 SPE-211391 gamma-model GC-error taxonomy

collaborators.Kameshwar Singh: PERA. Long-running BOPVT and multi-zone EOS collaborator

summary_for_rag: Singh, Mantzatzis, Whitson & Benjemia 2011 SPE-143612 is a field-application paper for an OMV Tunisia well with four zones (near-critical oil to lean gas condensate), nine non-communicating layers, and six MDT samples contaminated with oil-based mud at 16-78 wt% STO. Three transferable Curtis workflows: (1) MDT OBM-decontamination via iterative gamma-exponential fit, (2) zone and missing-layer composition reconstruction from clean-up-test OGR with mobility-weighted mixing, (3) LBC oil-viscosity tuning via per-SCN Zc anchored to Orrick-Erbar plus a single common C7+ multiplier. Single 34-component EOS pseudoised to 15 components covers all four zones.

title: Backpressure Equation for Layered Gas Reservoirs
journal: SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition, Denver
key_findings: Layered gas-reservoir BPE preserves single-layer Fetkovich form q_g = C_R (p_c_bar^2 - p_w^2) with PI-weighted pressure p_c_bar^2 = sum(C_Rl p_cl^2)/C_R and total PI C_R = sum(C_Rl); backflow during shut-ins embedded in derivation

Coupled with per-layer material balance (p_c/z)_l = (p_cl/z_il)(1 - G_p/G)_l forecasts differential depletion; Buf #3 Hugoton three-layer (Herrington 0.1 md, Krider 9.0 md, Winfield 3.3 md) matched 1967-2009 annual shut-in pressures; 1989 independent layer-pressure test matched without refitting

Long-term BPE monitoring with consistent shut-in durations detects tubing/skin deterioration via log-log shift below the established line; robust to partial/full layer communication (transmissibility 0-1 all on same line) and to test-condition deviations from normal operation
Four explicit validity limits: k > ~0.01 mD, pressure-squared regime, no significant rate-dependent skin (layered Forchheimer remained an open problem), PSS on shut-in timescale
contributed_to: Operator routine annual deliverability-test monitoring for layered Hugoton/Permian gas wells
Whitson-Rahmawati-Juell 2012 SPE-153073 cyclic-shut-in control
Knudsen-Foss-Whitson-Conn 2012 ADCHEM12 MILP scheduled shut-ins
Golan-Whitson Well Performance textbook canonical layered-system reference

collaborators.Aleksander Oma Juell: NTNU M.Sc. then PhD under Curtis. Three-paper sequence on layered-gas-well monitoring and optimisation

summary_for_rag: Juell & Whitson 2011 SPE-146066 derives a multi-layer backpressure equation for layered gas reservoirs with or without inter-layer communication. The headline result is that the layered BPE collapses to the single-layer Fetkovich form provided the layer constants and shut-in pressures are correctly averaged - PI-weighted pressure p_c_bar^2 is the well-felt shut-in pressure and total PI C_R is the sum. Coupled with per-layer material balance, it forecasts differential depletion. Validated against numerical simulation and against 1967-2009 Hugoton Buf #3 three-layer field data. Four explicit validity limits identified. Becomes the layered-system backpressure-analysis reference for subsequent Whitson work.

title: Modeling SAGD with a Black-Oil Proxy
journal: SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition, Denver
key_findings: SAGD modelled with isothermal black-oil simulator (SENSOR) as proxy for thermal simulator (STARS) using Rs as equivalent temperature surrogate: Rs=0 cold oil, Rs*=1 scf/STB fully-heated; gas component = steam at T*; edge oil-flow zone emulated as narrow Rs gradient band
Pseudo-oil-viscosity equation ln(mu_o/mu_oi) = A (p_b^-n - p_bi^-n) with single fit parameter n (deliberately empirical surrogate not physical correlation); n~1.8 for base case
Steam shrinkage factor SSF~0.7 accounts for ~30% steam condensation in reservoir that isothermal BO cannot model directly

Optimal n portable across injection rate (40-80 bbl/D), injector-producer offset (13-23 ft), heterogeneity (three Tachet 2009 slices spanning 0-10000 md), and 2D vs 3D geometry; depends on end-point viscosity (1.8-2.6 for mu_o* 4-12 cp) and heat-loss treatment (1.5 with heat loss vs 1.8 without)

BO proxy 8-12x faster than thermal simulator (91 s vs 1089 s for 2D with heat loss); enables 800-run SAGD optimization campaigns over injector/producer placement, pattern spacing, injection rate, steam temperature
builds_on: Modified black-oil framework (Cook 1974, Whitson-da Silva-Soreide 1988 SPE-18315, Whitson-Brule 2000 Monograph Chapter 7)
SENSOR BO simulator (Coats Engineering) as proxy
contributed_to: Full Ghasemi-Whitson SAGD series (2011, 2013, 2014, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018) extending the proxy to SA-SAGD and other thermal processes
Operator SAGD design optimization for Athabasca/Cold Lake/McMurray bitumen settings
Ghasemi-Whitson 2014 IPTC-18033 thermal-solvent SAGD with HYSYS coupling
Knudsen-Foss-Whitson-Conn 2012 ADCHEM12 shale-gas proxy (same proxy-design pattern, different physics)

collaborators.Mohsen Ghasemi: NTNU PhD then postdoc with Curtis 2011-2021. Lead author on seven-paper SAGD/heavy-oil/chalk-CO2 series

summary_for_rag: Ghasemi & Whitson 2011 SPE-147072 (Mohsen Ghasemi's NTNU PhD work funded by VISTA/Statoil) introduces a novel methodology for modelling SAGD heavy-oil processes using an isothermal black-oil reservoir simulator as a proxy for a full-physics thermal simulator. The core insight is that the steam-heating-driven viscosity reduction at the edge oil-flow zone can be emulated by using solution gas-oil ratio Rs as an equivalent temperature variable. Single fit parameter n in the pseudo-oil-viscosity equation; steam shrinkage factor SSF~0.7 for condensation; n portable across rate/geometry/heterogeneity. 8-12x CPU speedup enables 800-run SAGD optimization campaigns. First of seven Ghasemi-Whitson papers on the proxy framework.

title: Target-rate Tracking for Shale-gas Multi-well Pads by Scheduled Shut-ins
journal: 8th IFAC Symposium on Advanced Control of Chemical Processes (ADCHEM), Singapore
key_findings: Three-region radial composite proxy (high-k fracture, intermediate transition, low-k matrix) tuned against high-fidelity SENSOR multi-fracture reference over 70-day horizon with two predefined shut-ins; transition region regularises ~10^6 permeability contrast that destabilises pure dual-permeability models

MILP scheduling formulation jointly avoids liquid loading and tracks midstream target rate via binary choke-opening variables; exact linear reformulation of well-model min-operator and bilinear alpha*m_1 via big-M; reservoir radial-composite proxy embedded as explicit constraints

Turner-rate disjunctive constraint q_gc(p_w) alpha_k <= q_k requires no water saturation in reservoir model - liquid-loading captured as wellhead boundary condition; Floudas 1995 technique embeds disjunctive boundary in MILP without adding binary variables

Eight-well 28-day numerical example (4234 continuous + 456 binary vars, 5701 constraints): 4.1% higher cumulative gas than naive constant-week shut-in heuristic; much less erratic pad-total rate around target
builds_on: Whitson-Rahmawati-Juell 2012 SPE-153073 cyclic-shut-in control (the cyclic concept being scheduled and multi-well-extended)
Turner et al 1969 critical-velocity liquid-loading correlation
contributed_to: Operator shale-gas pad-management workflows for plant-level production scheduling
Hoffmann-Astutik-Rasmussen-Whitson 2016 IAM optimization (extends MILP-with-optimality-guarantee from binary-decision shut-ins to continuous-decision diluent + choke)
Knudsen-Whitson-Foss 2014 follow-up on shale-gas-power-production scheduling
Carlsen NRTA series (radial composite proxy concept reappears in fracture-stimulated-region modelling)

collaborators.Brage Rugstad Knudsen: NTNU PhD student under Bjarne Foss; shale-gas optimisation
collaborators.Bjarne Foss: NTNU Engineering Cybernetics. Optimisation expertise. Also Rahmawati-Whitson-Foss-Kuntadi 2010 SPE-130768 IAM co-author
collaborators.A. R. Conn: IBM T. J. Watson Research. Optimisation-theory input

summary_for_rag: Knudsen, Foss, Whitson & Conn 2012 ADCHEM12 extends Curtis's cyclic-shut-in concept (Whitson-Rahmawati-Juell 2012 SPE-153073) from single-well constant cycles to multi-well pad scheduling with target-rate tracking. The scheduling problem is cast as a mixed-integer linear program with binary choke-opening variables and an exact linear reformulation of the nonlinear well-model min-operator via big-M. Reservoir side is a three-region radial composite proxy (high-k fracture, intermediate transition, low-k matrix) tuned against a high-fidelity SENSOR multi-fracture simulation. Turner-rate disjunctive constraint captures liquid-loading as a wellhead boundary condition without water saturation in the reservoir model. Eight-well 28-day example: 4.1% higher cumulative gas than naive constant shut-in heuristic.

title: Cyclic Shut-In Eliminates Liquid-Loading in Gas Wells
journal: SPE/EAGE European Unconventional Resources Conference, Vienna
key_findings: Cyclic shut-in control rule: when well rate falls to Turner-like q_LL, rate-controlled wellhead device shuts well in for ~1 hr then re-opens; cycle continues until economic cutoff; no downhole equipment needed

Short cyclic shut-ins (~1 hr) preserve approximately 100% of continuously-unloaded ideal recovery (E_RF~100%); vertical well 1-day shut-ins 95%, 10-day 85%; HMF well 30-day shut-ins still >95% (much more tolerant due to per-fracture drainage)

Permeability-bounded window where CSC uplift is material: vertical 0.01-1 mD, HMF 5e-5 to 5e-3 mD; below: no recovery anyway, above: fast depletion before loading - peak uplift at k~0.03 mD vertical and k~3e-4 mD HMF

CSC works in layered no-crossflow systems with significant differential depletion: two-layer 0.785 md / 0.0523 md case at 433 psia layer differential matches continuously-unloaded ideal; 1-hr shut-in timescale is short relative to inter-layer crossflow timescale
Mechanism: wells still producing while shut-in at surface - gas continues to flow into near-well region and charge wellbore; reservoir doesn't recognise brief surface shut-in
Free-liquid-case redefinition: replace Turner rate with Gray-correlation-based pressure-drop excess over single-phase gas; plunger-lift installations should switch trigger to liquid-loading rate
builds_on: Turner et al 1969 / Coleman 1991 / Nosseir 2000 / Guo 2006 minimum-rate-to-lift correlations
Whitson-Sunjerga 2012 SPE-155499 LRS PVT framework for HMF examples
Gray correlation multiphase tubing pressure drop
contributed_to: Knudsen-Foss-Whitson-Conn 2012 ADCHEM12 MILP scheduling formulation (operational extension)
Operator-side wellhead CSC device deployment in Marcellus, Haynesville, Eagle Ford and other low-permeability shale-gas plays
Plunger-lift trigger-redesign recommendations
Carlsen-Whitson NRTA series
Whitson Academy course material on shale-gas operations

collaborators.Silvya Dewi Rahmawati: NTNU PhD student under Curtis and Bjarne Foss. Co-author on 2009 constant-shut-in precursor and 2010 SPE-130768 multi-field IAM benchmark
collaborators.Aleksander Oma Juell: NTNU PhD student under Curtis. Three-paper sequence with Curtis on layered and cyclic-shut-in gas-well operations

summary_for_rag: Whitson, Rahmawati & Juell 2012 SPE-153073 is the seminal Whitson-first-author paper on cyclic shut-in control (CSC) for low-permeability stimulated gas wells. The proposal is radical in its simplicity: when a well's rate falls to the Turner-like liquid-loading rate, a rate-controlled wellhead device automatically shuts the well in for ~1 hour then re-opens. The counter-intuitive headline result: short cyclic shut-ins preserve approximately 100% of the recovery of a hypothetical continuously-unloaded ideal well. Permeability-bounded window where uplift is material: 0.01-1 mD vertical, 5e-5 to 5e-3 mD HMF. Works in layered no-crossflow systems with differential depletion. Conceptual foundation for the Knudsen-Foss-Whitson-Conn 2012 ADCHEM12 MILP-scheduling extension.

title: Dynamic Delumping of Reservoir Simulation
journal: SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition, San Antonio
key_findings: BOz delumping with alpha-parameter tracer-tracked injection-gas handling: black-oil simulator output to detailed-EOS composition at well-connection level per time step, 20-80x faster than detailed-EOS simulation
z2z compositional delumping via lumped-to-detailed split factors derived from a single CCE on the detailed-EOS model, ~3x faster than detailed-EOS simulation
Key-component beta estimation and tracer tracking are operationally-equivalent approaches for injection-gas stream splitting
Whitson 1983 gamma-distribution fallback for C7+ when lumped-detailed consistency is broken
Extends the Suryanto-Ekstrand-Whitson-Uleberg-Hoda 2003 static split-factor matrix to dynamic per-timestep well-connection-level conversion
contributed_to: Pipe-It / Streamz IAM workflow
Hoda-Whitson 2013 SPE-164334 measurement-side rate conversion
Operator IAM workflows for gas injection in multi-reservoir developments
Khan et al. 2025 multi-source tracer-option workflow
summary_for_rag: Kuntadi, Whitson and Hoda 2012 SPE-159400 deliver the dynamic stream-conversion stack for integrated asset management: BOz delumping converts black-oil simulator output to detailed-EOS composition at well-connection level per timestep with proper injection-gas handling via alpha-parameter tracer tracking, and z2z delumping converts lumped-EOS output via phase-specific pressure-dependent split factors derived from a single CCE. BOz runs 20-80x faster than detailed-EOS, z2z about 3x faster. Underpins Pipe-It/Streamz workflows.

title: Well Test Rate Conversion to Compositional Wellstream
journal: SPE Middle East Oil and Gas Show and Exhibition, Manama, Bahrain
key_findings: Non-regression rate-to-composition conversion via single EOS flash of a seed-feed at the measured separator (p,T); the recovered wellstream reproduces measured GOR exactly with no iteration
Seed-feed weak-dependency principle: equilibrium K-values are weak functions of overall composition at fixed (p,T), so any reasonable seed-feed estimate works
OGR-based 5-node piecewise-linear lookup tables outperform GOR-based tables because wellstream composition is nearly linear in OGR
Constant shrinkage factor causes up to 50% oil-rate error when separator temperature varies
Hundreds of thousands of conversions per minute enables 2500-point online-meter automation and 100-well multi-field asset allocation
contributed_to: Pipe-It / Streamz online-meter and allocation modules
Operator multi-field asset-allocation workflows
Kuntadi-Whitson-Hoda 2012 SPE-159400 dynamic delumping (simulator-side complement)
Khan et al. 2025 multi-source tracer workflow
summary_for_rag: Hoda and Whitson 2013 SPE-164334 present the canonical Whitson-group method for converting measured well-test separator gas and oil volumetric rates into a molar compositional wellstream rate. A single EOS flash of a coarse seed-feed at the measured separator (p,T) gives exact non-iterative recovery of wellstream composition. OGR-based 5-node lookup tables make the method practical for multi-field asset allocation and online-meter automation. The method has been used at Petrostreamz since 2001 and underpins Pipe-It / Streamz.

title: Phase Behaviour and Viscosity Modelling of Athabasca Bitumen and Light Solvent Mixtures
journal: SPE Heavy Oil Conference Canada, Calgary
key_findings: 89-component EOSSCN characterisation extending to C90+, built from publicly-available Alberta crude assay data via Whitson 1983 gamma + modified Jacoby + Soreide + Twu with f_Twu=1
Modified Jacoby gamma-M correlation outperforms Soreide for ultra-heavy bitumen (RMS 0.574% vs 1.393%)
Fixed BIPs plus ±10% solvent-amount adjustment as the regression strategy keeps tuning simple and physically defensible
Modified Twu viscosity correlation plus linear Zc(Tb) modification brings dead-bitumen viscosity from 10^8 cp down to measured 24,471 cp at 100°F

Novel L&H splitting of C90+ into lower-viscous and higher-viscous fractions with identical EOS properties but distinct critical Z-factors reproduces solvent-saturated bitumen viscosity where EOS5/LBC failed
Six-component EOS6/LBC model predicts measured PVT to within 10% for N2/CO/CO2/C1/C2-saturated bitumen over 20-118°C, extrapolating to 350°C
builds_on: Mehrotra-Svrcek and Svrcek-Mehrotra 1982/1985 bitumen-with-solvent PVT data
contributed_to: Ghasemi-Whitson SAGD series 2014, 2015, 2016, 2017
Ghasemi-Whitson 2014 IPTC-18033 thermal-solvent SAGD with HYSYS surface coupling
Ghasemi-Whitson 2016/2017 CO2-EOR chalk papers (C7+ characterisation methodology)
Operator SA-SAGD design optimisation for Athabasca, Cold Lake, Peace River
Whitson Academy course material on ultra-heavy-oil PVT
summary_for_rag: Ghasemi and Whitson 2013 SPE-165416 deliver the upstream fluid model needed for solvent-augmented SAGD (SA-SAGD). 89-component PR79 EOS with LBC viscosity characterisation of Athabasca bitumen built from publicly-available crude-assay data via Whitson 1983 gamma + modified Jacoby + Soreide + Twu (f_Twu=1) chain. The novel L&H splitting of C90+ into lower-viscous and higher-viscous fractions reproduces solvent-saturated bitumen viscosity where the unmodified EOS/LBC failed. Validated against bitumen-with-pure-solvent PVT data over 20-118°C with extrapolation to 350°C thermal-process conditions. Completes the PVT stack underneath the Ghasemi-Whitson 2011 BO-proxy-for-SAGD framework.

title: Optimised Well Modelling of Liquid-Rich Shale Reservoirs
journal: SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition, New Orleans
key_findings: Half-fracture symmetry-element FD model with system-specific grid refinement: 80x20 cells for oil LRS, much coarser for near-saturated GC, 200x40 for highly undersaturated GC; ~40x CPU speedup at 20 fractures
Treatment-fluid injection and flowback via stress-dependent transmissibility (after Jurus-Whitson-Golan 2013) plus water capillary pressure
Bottomhole pressure gauge installation for first 2-4 weeks of LRS production is high-ROI: multi-phase correlations diverge by up to 3000 psi (~40%) when water cut is high

Three key history-match parameters: matrix permeability k, fracture half-length xf, initial in-situ fluid composition; productivity term xf*sqrt(k) is non-unique without independent fracture diagnostics
Optimal drawdown for highly-undersaturated GC: p_wf ≈ p_sat in period 1 (100-170 days), then ramp down; reverse-sequence strategies permanently sacrifice mobility
NPV optimum ~45 fractures for oil LRS, ~30 for GC LRS; fracture area dominates over distribution along the wellbore
contributed_to: Operator LRS field development optimisation in Eagle Ford, Bakken, Avalon, Montney
Carlsen-Bowie-Dahouk-Mydland-Whitson-Yusra 2021 SPE-205884 NRTA workflow
Mydland-Whitson liquid-rich-shale series 2020-2021
Whitson Academy LRS course material
2023 AIME Honorary Membership citation for influential consulting
summary_for_rag: Juell and Whitson 2013 SPE-166380 integrate single-well finite-difference modelling with economic optimisation for liquid-rich shale (LRS) wells across Eagle Ford, Bakken, Avalon, and Montney applications. Six methodological contributions span half-fracture symmetry-element FD models with system-specific grid refinement, treatment-fluid injection and flowback via stress-dependent transmissibility plus water capillary pressure, bottomhole-pressure gauging during the first 2-4 weeks of LRS production, three key history-match parameters with xf*sqrt(k) non-uniqueness, revenue-optimal two-period drawdown control for highly-undersaturated GC (p_wf ≈ p_sat then ramp down), and NPV optimisation showing ~45 fractures optimal for oil and ~30 for GC with fracture area dominating over distribution.

title: Modelling Water Flow in Hydraulically-Fractured Shale Wells
journal: SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition, New Orleans
key_findings: Stress-dependent permeability multiplier k/k0 = 10^(m*p_net) admits physical injection volume in conventional black-oil simulator without explicit fracture-mechanics physics
Four-parameter imbibition capillary-pressure shape with P_c,max ~3480 psia at nanometre pores from Young-Laplace; capillary forces are first-order driver, not second-order correction
Capillary forces dominate water retention: cumulative recovery ranges from ~80% (no Pc) to ~20% (strong b3=3); long-term gas production essentially unaffected
Post-fracturing shut-in via capillary-driven imbibition reduces water recovery (~28% at 0 days to ~5% at 100+ days) and enhances initial gas rates up to 9x peak
Parameter-separation matrix decouples water-recovery, gas-production, and injectivity sensitivities for history-matching; dry shales (S_wi<S_wc) trap more water but have over 2x cumulative gas
builds_on: Young-Laplace equation for nanometre-pore capillary pressure
King 2012 operator observations on post-fracturing shut-in
contributed_to: Juell-Whitson 2013 SPE-166380 LRS well modelling (cites this paper as origin of stress-dependent multiplier)
Mydland-Whitson LRS series 2020-2021
Operator shale-gas completion-design and flowback-management workflows
Post-fracturing shut-in adoption in Marcellus, Haynesville, Barnett
Whitson Academy hydraulic fracturing course material
collaborators: Michael Golan (NTNU production engineering, Curtis's Well Performance co-author)
summary_for_rag: Jurus, Whitson and Golan 2013 SPE-166439 model hydraulic-fracture water injection, post-fracturing shut-in, and flowback in shale gas wells using a conventional black-oil FD simulator (SENSOR) augmented by a stress-dependent permeability multiplier k/k0 = 10^(m*p_net) and a four-parameter imbibition capillary-pressure curve. Capillary forces (P_c,max ~3480 psia from Young-Laplace at nanometre pores) dominate water retention: cumulative water recovery spans ~20-80% depending on curve shape with negligible long-term effect on gas production. Post-fracturing shut-in drives water deeper into the matrix via capillary imbibition, reducing flowback and boosting initial gas rates up to 9x peak. Origin paper for the stress-dependent transmissibility multiplier used in Juell-Whitson 2013 LRS modelling workflow.

title: Numerical Investigation and Integrated Optimisation of Solvent-SAGD Process
journal: International Petroleum Technology Conference, Kuala Lumpur
key_findings: Dual-mechanism solvent classification by K-value at steam saturation: medium solvents C5-C8 (K~1) condense at the steam-oil interface and dilute bitumen viscosity; heavy solvents C12, C15 (K~0.02) condense inside the steam chamber and develop in-chamber micro-mobility ~0.6 md/cp
Optimum solvent molecular weight 100-120 (between C7 and C8) from the intersection of two mechanism-degradation trends
Non-monotonic heavy-solvent concentration response: C15 plateaus above 20 vol% (excess back-produced) while C6 improves monotonically up to 40 vol%
Strong relative-permeability dependence: straight-line relperm (n=1) lifts C8-SAGD ultimate recovery to ~100%; krg(Swc) affects heavy-solvent recovery but not medium-solvent recovery

Full IAM optimisation across STARS reservoir + separator + steam generator + 100 km HYSYS pipeline: optimum C6 volume fraction 0.15 at p_inj=2300 kPa, 0.20 at 4000 kPa; optimum pipeline mixture viscosity 150 cp
builds_on: Ghasemi-Whitson 2013 SPE-165416 EOSSCN/EOS5/EOS6/LBC fluid model for bitumen with solvents
contributed_to: Ghasemi-Whitson 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018 SAGD series
Operator SA-SAGD design for Athabasca, Cold Lake, Peace River, Orinoco
Whitson Academy thermal-process course material
Hoffmann-Astutik-Rasmussen-Whitson 2016 IAM optimisation pattern reuse
summary_for_rag: Ghasemi and Whitson 2014 IPTC-18033 combine the 2011 BO-proxy-for-SAGD framework with the 2013 bitumen+solvent EOS model into a full compositional-thermal study of hybrid solvent-SAGD via CMG STARS. Central diagnostic: K-value at SAGD steam conditions (2300 kPa, 219°C) determines where the solvent condenses and thus the recovery mechanism - medium solvents (K~1) condense at the steam-oil interface and dilute viscosity; heavy solvents (K~0.02) condense inside the chamber and develop in-chamber micro-mobility. Optimum solvent molecular weight 100-120. Full integrated-asset optimisation including 100km HYSYS pipeline gives optimum C6 fraction 0.15 at injection pressure 2300 kPa.

title: Shale-Gas Scheduling for Natural-Gas Supply in Electric Power Production
journal: Energy (Elsevier), Vol. 78, pp. 165-182
key_findings: Shale-gas reservoirs as ad-hoc distributed gas storage: 10-15% shut-in time reduces 10-year recovery by less than 3% in ultra-tight rock (k<0.01 md)
Firm-contract value chain bypasses LDC storage markup; 12.2% profit uplift for 105-well/400 MW NGPP case with 4-months-ahead NYMEX firm pricing
Lagrangian-relaxation decomposition with primal-recovery heuristic makes field-scale (105 wells, 9030 binaries, 56519 constraints) MILP tractable in 15-25 CPU minutes per receding-horizon iteration
Receding-horizon economic MPC with spot-price discount factor xi handles day-ahead vs far-horizon price uncertainty
Profit-uplift scales with firm-contract duration: 12.2% (4 months), 10.2% (3 months), 5.9% (2 months), 2.2% (1 month)
contributed_to: Operator shale-gas field-management workflows linking production scheduling with firm-contract sales
Knudsen NTNU PhD thesis
Hoffmann-Astutik-Rasmussen-Whitson 2016 IAM optimisation
Gas-power coupled optimisation literature for renewable-heavy grids
collaborators: Brage Rugstad Knudsen (NTNU PhD under Foss)
summary_for_rag: Knudsen, Whitson and Foss 2014 (Energy Vol. 78, pp. 165-182) propose shale-gas reservoirs as ad-hoc distributed gas storage for firm-contract supply to natural-gas power plants (NGPPs), bypassing Local Distribution Company storage. Physical basis: in ultra-tight shale (k<0.01 md) shut-ins totalling 10-15% of operating time reduce 10-year recovery by less than 3%. Field-scale MILP (105 wells, 400 MW NGPP) made tractable via Lagrangian-relaxation decomposition with primal-recovery heuristic. Wrapped in receding-horizon economic MPC with daily re-optimisation over 4-week horizon. 12.2% profit uplift over continuous-production base case with 4-months-ahead NYMEX firm pricing.

title: Liquid-Rich Shale Versus Conventional Depletion Performance
journal: SPE/EAGE European Unconventional Conference and Exhibition, Vienna
key_findings: Revenue ratio R(365d)/R(10d) as universal permeability classifier: ultra-tight asymptote ~7, conventional asymptote ~36.5, transition ~1.5-2 log cycles wide
Quantitative permeability classification: conventional behaviour k>~0.5 md, ultra-tight k<1-5e-3 md

Highly-undersaturated systems preserve conventional behaviour deeper into tight rock: undersaturated GC behaves conventionally to k=0.05 md; undersaturated moderate-GOR oil shows constant producing GOR at k=0.2-1.0 md
Explicit recommendation against average reservoir pressure and recovery factor as LRS performance metrics (drainage area is arbitrary in ultra-tight)
Analytical revenue-ratio asymptotes derivable from single-phase dimensionless-rate solutions without simulation
contributed_to: Operator well-screening workflows for Eagle Ford, Bakken, Avalon, Wolfcamp
Carlsen et al. NRTA series 2019-2021
Mydland-Whitson LRS series 2020-2021
Whitson Academy unconventional reservoir classification material
summary_for_rag: Lei, Cheng and Whitson 2014 SPE-167788 provide systematic permeability-space mapping for liquid-rich reservoirs spanning 10 nd to 100 md across four fluid systems (rich GC, near-critical oil, highly-undersaturated GC, moderate-GOR oil). The dimensionless revenue ratio R(365d)/R(10d) classifies depletion regime: ~7 for ultra-tight, ~36.5 for conventional, with ~1.5-2 log cycles transition. Conventional behaviour above k~0.5 md; ultra-tight below k~1-5e-3 md. Highly-undersaturated systems preserve conventional behaviour an extra log cycle into tight rock due to dominant single-phase flow. The revenue ratio sidesteps the arbitrary drainage-area assumption that breaks average pressure and recovery factor as LRS metrics.

title: Global Component Lumping for EOS Calculations
journal: SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition, Amsterdam
key_findings: Combinatorial enumeration of all feasible contiguous-lumping scenarios: 8855 (EOSx 15), 142506 (EOSx 9), 237336 (EOSx 6) for an EOSxx(34) PR-EOS reference
Weighted RMS evaluated against ~1200 simulated PVT points from the detailed reference EOS (not against measured data) gives monotonic-decrease-with-Nx property
Two-stage MMP-aware filter computes MMP only for 100-500 lowest-RMS scenarios; EOSx(9) achieves 1.265% RMS with MMP within 0.4 psi of detailed model
Unexpected finding: best-MMP EOSx(9) and EOSx(6) scenarios did NOT preserve C30+ as heaviest fraction but lumped it with C24+-C26+; heavy-fraction lumping helps MMP accuracy

Three user-configurable constraints (light-pure-component restrictions, contiguous NBP-ordered hydrocarbon lumping, no unconstrained partial lumping) make the search tractable and physically defensible
contributed_to: Kuntadi-Whitson-Hoda 2012 z2z delumping workflow (assumes carefully-lumped EOS)
Operator EOS-reduction workflows for compositional reservoir simulation, flowline transient modelling, surface process simulation
Subsequent NTNU and whitson EOS-pseudoisation studies for LRS, CO2-EOR, underground hydrogen storage
Whitson Academy EOS characterisation course material
summary_for_rag: Alavian, Whitson and Martinsen 2014 SPE-170912 transform EOS component lumping from empirical heuristic art into systematic automated global search. Three innovations: combinatorial enumeration of all contiguous-lumping scenarios (formula N_EOSx = (N_xx-1)!/[(N_xx-N_x)!(N_x-1)!]) scaling to ~10^5 scenarios tractable by overnight batch via Matsumoto Ruby script; weighted RMS quality measured against the detailed reference EOS rather than measured data (gives monotonic-decrease-with-Nx property); two-stage MMP filter applied only to lowest-RMS scenarios. Implemented in PhazeComp + Pipe-It/Streamz. Examples reduce EOSxx(34) PR-EOS+LBC to EOSx(15), EOSx(9), EOSx(6). Unexpected finding: best-MMP scenarios for EOSx(9) and EOSx(6) lump C30+ with C24+-C26+ rather than preserving it as heaviest fraction.

title: Compositional Variation in SAGD
journal: SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition, Houston
key_findings: Steam-base K-values via Søreide-Whitson 1992 alpha_H2O correction with iterative omega-update workflow; sharp K-value discontinuity at steam saturation temperature; operationally SAGD simulation matches CO2-base K-value simulation

Vaporising-Condensing Mechanism (VCM): light bitumen fractions vaporise at the steam-oil interface and condense at the edge oil-flow zone, enriching the interface and lowering local viscosity; ~1% recovery gain for 8°API Athabasca but ~10-12% for 14-18°API upgraded heavy oils
Vertical viscosity-gradient correlation ln(mu_o/mu_o,top) = a*D^m with m=0.772 for biodegraded bitumen; yields ~1% faster chamber growth and amplifies VCM by ~3.5%
Bitumen pseudo-fraction count N=1/2/5 has negligible effect on pure-SAGD recovery for Athabasca but N=1 does not capture VCM; multi-fraction needed when VCM is material
builds_on: PhazeComp and Multiflash for K-value cross-validation
contributed_to: Ghasemi et al. 2016 and 2018 Ekofisk CO2-EOR papers
Operator SAGD design for upgraded/lighter heavy oils where VCM matters
Whitson Academy thermal-process course material
collaborators: Mohsen Ghasemi (NTNU PhD/postdoc with Curtis, then Petrostreamz)
summary_for_rag: Ghasemi and Whitson 2015 SPE-175022 consolidate the pure-SAGD compositional analysis, closing out the Ghasemi-Whitson SAGD series. Four contributions: steam-base K-values via Søreide-Whitson alpha_H2O correction with iterative omega-update (operationally matches the CO2-base approach because thermal simulators operate at steam saturation), the novel Vaporising-Condensing Mechanism (VCM) where light bitumen fractions vaporise at the steam-oil interface and condense at the cooler edge zone (~1% gain for 8°API Athabasca, ~10-12% for lighter heavy oils), a vertical viscosity-gradient correlation for biodegraded bitumen, and a pseudo-fraction-count sensitivity showing single-pseudo-component adequate for Athabasca but not for VCM-relevant cases.

title: Determining Diffusion Coefficients for CO2 Injection in Ekofisk-Oil Saturated Chalk Using a Constant Volume Diffusion Method
journal: SPE Improved Oil Recovery Conference, Tulsa
key_findings: Novel CVD apparatus measures multi-component CO2 diffusion coefficients in oil-saturated chalk at reservoir conditions (110°C, 257 bara) via 750-800 hour pressure decline
Oil-phase diffusion coefficients decrease with CO2 content (Stokes-Einstein-like); gas-phase increases until two-phase region develops then decreases
Three-mechanism coupling in CVD-live-oil (diffusion + density-driven convection + capillary damping) vs pure-diffusion CVD-STO; live-oil pressure decline ~10x faster than diffusion alone
Chalk permeability must be tight enough (~0.2 md) to minimise convection while allowing CO2 transport; k=5 md shows convection-dominated behaviour
ECLIPSE 300 cannot model compositional dependency of diffusion coefficients - flagged as simulator limitation requiring next-generation simulator development
builds_on: Sigmund 1976 diffusion correlation with da Silva-Belery 1989 extension
contributed_to: Ghasemi et al. 2018 SPEREE CO2-EOR follow-up
Ekofisk and other North Sea chalk CO2-EOR design workflows
Carbon-capture-and-storage modelling for chalk and tight carbonates
Whitson Academy diffusion-in-fractured-reservoirs course material
collaborators: V. S. Suicmez (Maersk Oil and Gas)
summary_for_rag: Ghasemi, Astutik, Alavian, Whitson, Siagalas, Olsen and Suicmez 2016 SPE-179550 develop a novel CVD (Constant-Volume Diffusion) apparatus to measure multi-component CO2 diffusion coefficients in real Ekofisk oil saturated chalk at reservoir conditions (110°C, 257 bara). Two experiments (CVD-STO and CVD-live-oil) separate three transport mechanisms: pure molecular diffusion (STO), diffusion + density-driven convection (live-oil with rising liquid density on CO2 dissolution), and capillary damping at the two-phase boundary. Oil-phase diffusion coefficients decrease with CO2 content (Stokes-Einstein-like); gas-phase diffusion shows a tipping point. Funded by the JCR7 joint-industry project.

title: Shale Reserve Forecasting - Model Consistency and Uncertainty
journal: SPE Europec featured at 78th EAGE Conference and Exhibition, Vienna
key_findings: Four-cycle rate-decline life-cycle for HZMF shale wells: Cycle 1 IA with decreasing FBHP, Cycle 2 IA with constant FBHP, Cycle 3 transition IA->BD, Cycle 4 BD with constant FBHP; each cycle maps to a different appropriate analysis method

HM-calibrates-RTA/DCA model-consistency framework: history-matched numerical model supplies the Arps b exponent, IA-to-BD transition time, material-balance relation, and dimensionless-solution tables that RTA/DCA apply to neighbouring non-HM'd wells

Threshold fracture conductivity kf*wf > 1000*k*xf for nano-darcy HZMF wells revises the Golan-Whitson 1986 conventional 1000 md-ft rule; in nano-darcy rock conductivities as low as 10-25 md-ft behave as infinite-conductivity

Non-uniqueness J_IA = A_f*sqrt(k) in linear IA flow generalises Juell-Whitson 2013 single-fracture xf*sqrt(k) non-uniqueness to multi-fracture well level - different combinations of fracture area and matrix permeability give identical decline
builds_on: Fetkovich 1980 generalised DCA with type curves
Lei-Cheng-Whitson 2014 SPE-167788 LRS vs conventional classification
Nobakht et al. 2013 composite-well transition behaviour
contributed_to: Operator shale-well EUR-forecasting workflows blending HM, RTA, and DCA
Carlsen et al. NRTA series 2019-2021
Mydland-Whitson LRS series 2020-2021
Carlsen-Bowie-Dahouk-Mydland-Whitson-Yusra 2021 SPE-205884 NRTA workflow
Whitson Academy shale-forecasting course material
Industry recognition contributing to 2023 AIME Honorary Membership
summary_for_rag: Whitson, Coll, Dahouk and Juell 2016 SPE-180140 bridge three shale reserve-forecasting paradigms - DCA, RTA, and numerical history matching - with a unified call for model consistency. Four contributions: four-cycle rate-decline life-cycle decomposition for HZMF wells (each cycle needs different analysis method), HM-calibrates-RTA/DCA framework (history-matched numerical model anchors the Arps b exponent, IA-to-BD transition time, material-balance relation, and dimensionless-solution tables for neighbouring wells), threshold fracture conductivity kf*wf > 1000*k*xf for nano-darcy regime (revising the Golan-Whitson 1986 conventional rule), and non-uniqueness J_IA = A_f*sqrt(k) in linear IA flow.

title: Diluent Injection Optimisation for a Heavy Oil Field
journal: SPE Heavy Oil Conference and Exhibition, Kuwait City
key_findings: MILP formulation with SOS2 piecewise-linear relaxation of two coupled non-linearities (well performance + ASTM D7152-11 viscosity mixing rule); solvable in 0.1-5 s per time step with global-optimum guarantee

Two-PVT-per-well model with DC-dependent upper leg: wellbore split at ESP intake into lower leg using reservoir-oil PVT and upper leg using diluted-oil PVT; Brinkman emulsion viscosity for oil-continuous flow with DC-shift of inversion point

Optimal DC allocation across 60 wells gives 50.8 MMsm3 cum reservoir oil (+6.3% vs base) with only 0.53 MMsm3 diluent vs 50.0 MMsm3 (+4.6%) and 4.57 MMsm3 for naive constant allocation - 9x less diluent for 1.6 points more recovery uplift

Diluent-type sensitivity: identical recovery across 45/49/51°API but very different demand (0.59/0.53/0.40 MMsm3) and flash loss (4-9 vol% lighter to heavier); selection must co-optimise market price + flash loss + viscosity efficacy
Doubles as Real-Time Production Optimisation (RTPO) tool integrating Hoda-Whitson 2013 well-test rate conversion and Kuntadi-Whitson-Hoda 2012 black-oil delumping
builds_on: Hoffmann 2014 NTNU MSc thesis on piecewise-linear short-term production optimisation
contributed_to: Operator RTPO systems for heavy-oil fields with ESP lift and diluent injection
whitson Pipe-It IAM product line
Whitson Academy heavy-oil production-optimisation course material
collaborators: A. Hoffmann (Petrostreamz; NTNU MSc under Curtis)
summary_for_rag: Hoffmann, Astutik, Rasmussen and Whitson 2016 SPE-184119 formulate diluent-allocation + choke-opening optimisation for a 60-well ESP-lifted heavy-oil field as a Mixed-Integer Linear Program with SOS2 piecewise-linear relaxation of the two coupled non-linearities (per-well performance curves and ASTM D7152-11 volumetric viscosity mixing rule). First Whitson MILP-optimisation paper applying SOS2 rather than binary shut-in scheduling. Two-PVT-per-well model with DC-dependent upper leg handles Brinkman emulsion viscosity and the inversion-point shift. Optimal allocation gives 9x less diluent for 1.6 points more recovery uplift vs naive constant allocation. Doubles as Real-Time Production Optimisation tool.

title: Tertiary-CO2 Flooding in a Composite Fractured-Chalk Reservoir
journal: Journal of Petroleum Science and Engineering, Vol. 160, pp. 327-340
key_findings: Composite fractured-chalk core apparatus extends single-whole-core CVD to 6-plug stacked geometry enabling study of capillary continuity, scale effect, and inter-plug heterogeneity
Water blockage reduces tertiary-CO2 diffusion linearly with post-waterflood water saturation; tertiary diffusion 3.4-6x smaller than secondary
Capillary continuity insignificant for tertiary-CO2 flooding when diffusion dominates (matrix Peclet ~0.01); contrasts gas-oil drainage but aligns with miscible-condition findings

Sulfate-driven wettability alteration drives 70% (seawater) vs 46-60% (sulfate-free) waterflood recovery on North Sea chalk per Austad et al. 2015 mechanism; higher WF recovery means more water blockage and less tertiary-CO2 uplift
builds_on: Ghasemi et al. 2016 SPE-179550 CVD apparatus
Ghasemi et al. 2017 SPE-185017 EAGE whole-core tertiary-CO2
Saidi 1987 / Horie 1990 / Ringen 2005 gas-oil drainage capillary continuity
Austad et al. 2015 sulfate-driven wettability alteration
contributed_to: Ghasemi et al. 2018 SPEREE SPE-180102-PA follow-up
Ekofisk and Tor field CO2-EOR design workflows
JCR consortium chalk-EOR methodology
Whitson Academy fractured-chalk EOR course material
collaborators: V. S. Suicmez (Maersk Oil and Gas)
summary_for_rag: Ghasemi, Astutik, Alavian, Whitson, Sigalas, Olsen and Suicmez 2017 JPSE extend the 2016 CVD apparatus from single whole-core to composite 6-plug stacked geometry, run as tertiary-CO2 after waterflood. Composite geometry enables study of capillary continuity, scale effect, and inter-plug heterogeneity. Four findings: water blockage reduces tertiary-CO2 diffusion linearly with post-WF Sw; capillary continuity insignificant when diffusion dominates (Pe~0.01); sulfate-driven wettability alteration drives 70% vs 46-60% WF recovery; higher WF recovery means more water blockage and less tertiary-CO2 uplift.

title: Synthetic Fluid Selection for High-Pressure Gas Condensate Flow Tests
journal: SPE Europec featured at 79th EAGE Conference and Exhibition, Paris
key_findings: Three-to-four component synthetic fluid design with C1 + light-intermediate alkane + Diphenylmethane (DPH-C1, C13H12, M=168.24) heavy aromatic
SRK EOS with zero BIPs as engineered simplification - tolerable for synthetic 3-4-component HC mixtures, removes a regression-variable axis
Triple pressure-dependent property design criterion (mu_g/mu_o, Vro/Vt, sigma_go) per Fevang-Whitson 1996 theory - design for dimensionless flow drivers not raw PVT
Twelve-fluid open-literature validation: lean (Arun 1.1%) to near-critical (NS-NCGC 45%); sweet to 34% H2S; 104-163°C; T_syn=50°C consistently best across all 12 cases
builds_on: Fevang-Whitson 1996 SPERE three-region gas-condensate flow theory
Published reservoir-fluid datasets (Arun, Khuff, NS-LGC, NS-NCGC, Brazeau, Caroline, SPE-56476)
contributed_to: PERA / whitson commercial flow-test design
Academic flow-test apparatus designs adopting DPH-C1 as heavy aromatic surrogate
Whitson Academy gas-condensate flow physics course material
Singh-Whitson collaboration continuity into 2023-2025 BOPVT and ADNOC papers
summary_for_rag: Singh and Whitson 2017 SPE-185791 document the PERA commercial methodology refined over 15 years across 20+ projects for designing synthetic gas condensate fluids that mimic reservoir PVT, viscosity, and IFT behaviour using 3-4 inexpensive non-toxic hydrocarbon compounds. Component slate: methane + light-intermediate alkane + Diphenylmethane heavy aromatic. SRK with zero BIPs. Triple pressure-dependent design criterion (mu_g/mu_o, Vro/Vt, sigma_go) per Fevang-Whitson 1996. Twelve-fluid validation across lean to near-critical, sweet to very sour, 104-163°C with T_syn=50°C consistently optimal.

title: Molar Distributions of Equilibrium Systems
journal: SPE Europec featured at 80th EAGE Conference and Exhibition, Copenhagen
key_findings: Gamma-shape invariance principle: in most equilibrium systems of conventional petroleum mixtures, C7+ molar distribution shape alpha is approximately invariant across equilibrium phases and close to the feed

Verified across three classes: P-T phase envelope, two-phase flash within envelope, and isothermal gravitational segregation; near-critical saturated GOC across 4000-10000 ft shows alpha 1.907-1.989 vs feed 1.933
Breakdown rule: invariance fails when K-values for light-heavy C7-C11 components exceed ~10 (high-T low-p conditions)
Operational application: single measured phase suffices to infer C7+ distribution shape of an unsampled equilibrium phase (un-sampled gas cap, pipeline gas)
contributed_to: Whitson-Alqahtani-Chuparova 2018 URTeC-2882502 well-box heterogeneity paper
Younus et al. 2019 URTeC-551 Field-wide EOS (common alpha gamma model)
Operator un-sampled gas-cap and pipeline-gas characterisation workflows
NTNU and whitson compositional-grading research
collaborators: Fahd Alqahtani (KSU PhD on sabbatical at NTNU)
summary_for_rag: Alqahtani and Whitson 2018 SPE-190797 establish the empirical gamma-shape invariance principle: in most equilibrium systems of conventional petroleum mixtures, the C7+ molar distribution shape (gamma alpha) of equilibrium phases is approximately invariant and very close to the feed. Verified across P-T phase envelope, two-phase flash, and isothermal gravitational segregation including a near-critical saturated GOC. Breakdown rule when K(C7-C11) exceeds 10. Operationally lets a single measured phase infer the C7+ shape of an unsampled equilibrium phase.

title: Laboratory Tests and Modelling of CO2 Injection in Chalk With Fracture/Matrix-Transport Mechanisms
journal: SPE Reservoir Evaluation and Engineering Vol. 21 No. 1, pp. 122-136
key_findings: Paired STO + n-C10 experimental design isolates diffusion mechanism: STO at 258.5 bar/110°C has 49% density contrast (gravity-convection active); n-C10 at 335.9 bar/100°C has <0.15% density difference (gravity suppressed, diffusion-dominated)
Time-varying diffusion-coefficient history-match via three sequential models stitched in Pipe-It workarounds ECLIPSE 300 constant-D limitation across STO 0-90% CO2 composition range

Peclet number plus viscous-gravity ratio diagnostic confirms diffusion-dominated matrix transport (Pe~0.001 STO, ~0.002 n-C10; K_l/D~1.005); R_v/g=0.21 explains 15% no-diffusion recovery as pure gravity drainage
Capillary pressure dominance differentiates STO (20% recovery swing from Pc=0 vs base) from n-C10 (negligible effect because CO2 and n-C10 are first-contact miscible)
builds_on: Ghasemi et al. 2016 SPE-179550 CVD apparatus
Ghasemi et al. 2017 JPSE composite-core extension
contributed_to: Ekofisk and Tor field CO2-EOR design workflows
Carbon-capture-and-storage modelling for chalk and tight carbonates
Three-number diagnostic framework reused in subsequent fractured-reservoir transport studies
Whitson Academy fractured-chalk EOR and lab experimental design course material
collaborators: V. S. Suicmez (Maersk Oil and Gas)
summary_for_rag: Ghasemi, Astutik, Alavian, Whitson, Sigalas, Olsen and Suicmez 2018 SPEREE SPE-180102-PA use a fracture/matrix flow cell with paired STO and n-C10 experiments to isolate the diffusion mechanism in chalk CO2 injection. The n-C10 experiment is density-matched (CO2 0.699 vs n-C10 0.698 g/cm3) which suppresses gravity convection and forces diffusion-dominated transport. Time-varying diffusion coefficient via three sequential models (Pipe-It-stitched) works around ECLIPSE 300 constant-D limitation across 0-90% CO2 composition. Peclet number + viscous-gravity ratio + capillary-pressure sensitivity together classify the dominant transport mechanism.

title: Fluid Heterogeneity on a Well-Box Scale in Tight Unconventional Reservoirs
journal: Unconventional Resources Technology Conference, Houston
key_findings: Well-box concept and dimensional envelope: 5000-10000 ft lateral, 100-1000 ft fracture height, 10-500 million bbl bulk volume, 0.5-50 million bbl HCPV
Complete-recovery fractions: dry gas 50-80% IGIP, gas condensate 5-30% ICIP, oil with solution-gas drive 10-20% IOIP

Parasequence-based fluid heterogeneity architecture: vertical fluid-composition variation tied to parasequence stacking + lateral k/phi heterogeneity within each parasequence, captured via Dykstra-Parsons V_k + V_phi + V_ri with four correlation scenarios
OGR(t) similarity-screening methodology for identifying most-probable well-box heterogeneity realisations from basin-wide production data
contributed_to: Mydland-Whitson LRS series 2020-2021
Carlsen-Whitson NRTA series 2019-2021
Younus et al. 2019 URTeC-551 Field-wide EOS
Operator basin-development screening workflows
Whitson Academy LRS heterogeneity course material
collaborators: Fahd Alqahtani (KSU PhD on sabbatical at NTNU)
summary_for_rag: Whitson, Alqahtani and Chuparova 2018 URTeC-2882502 is a Whitson-first-author prospectus paper proposing the well-box scale (well-lifetime drainage volume) as the right scale at which fluid-rock heterogeneity affects LRS well performance. Three contributions: well-box concept + dimensional envelope + complete-recovery benchmarks; parasequence-based heterogeneity hypothesis with Dykstra-Parsons-generalised statistical framework (4 correlation scenarios over k, phi, OGR); OGR(t) similarity-screening methodology using basin-wide production data. Explicitly a call-to-arms for multi-disciplinary collaboration. Vocabulary has been adopted by subsequent NTNU/whitson LRS work.

title: Fluid Sampling in Tight Unconventionals
journal: SPE Annual Technical Conference and Exhibition, Calgary
key_findings: In-situ representative vs reservoir representative sample classification: in-situ representative requires volume-weighted average of OOIP/OGIP; reservoir representative requires only non-contamination and reservoir origin

EOS-development misconception correction: any uncontaminated sample can and should be used to build an EOS model; industry routinely discards millions of dollars of usable PVT data each year because of the misconception

Four-fluid-type MFHW depletion signature catalogues evolution of wellstream properties for black oil, volatile oil, near-critical volatile oil, and near-critical gas condensate under both undersaturated and saturated initial conditions; identifies the elevated-saturation-pressure phenomenon explained as standard swelling-test physics

ECM1 updated procedure for initially undersaturated LRS (4-step using known Rsi=Rpi as matching parameter); reconstruction matches reservoir fluid within +/-0.5% C7+ error vs 5-15% for naive recombination
ECM2 updated procedure for initially saturated LRS (two variants for p_sat > p_i and p_sat < p_i)
builds_on: Younus et al. 2019 URTeC-551 Field-wide EOS (companion paper)
contributed_to: Operator LRS sampling and EOS-development workflows in Eagle Ford, Bakken, Permian, Montney
Mydland-Whitson LRS series 2020-2021
Carlsen et al. 2020-2022 NRTA papers
Singh-Whitson-Nielsen 2025 SPE-225382 BOPVT vs lab-PVT
Whitson Academy LRS sampling course material
Industry recognition contributing to 2023 AIME Honorary Membership
collaborators: Mathias Lia Carlsen (Whitson AS senior engineer)
Stian Mydland (Whitson AS engineer; NTNU PhD)
summary_for_rag: Carlsen, Whitson, Alavian, Martinsen, Mydland, Singh, Younus and Yusra 2019 SPE-196056 distil Whitson-group experience into a tight-unconventional sampling methodology. Headline reframing: in-situ representative (volume-weighted average of OOIP/OGIP) vs reservoir representative (any uncontaminated produced sample). Industry routinely discards millions of dollars of usable PVT data because of the misconception that only in-situ representative samples can be used for EOS development. Four-fluid-type MFHW depletion signature explains the elevated-saturation-pressure phenomenon. ECM1 (4-step undersaturated) and ECM2 (two-variant saturated) update Fevang-Whitson 1994 ECM for LRS, achieving +/-0.5% C7+ reconstruction error vs 5-15% for naive recombination.

title: Compositional Tracking of a Huff-n-Puff Project in the Eagle Ford
journal: Unconventional Resources Technology Conference, Denver
key_findings: HnP PVT experiment (CVI-CVD hybrid) captures the cyclic huff-puff structure that conventional displacement-focused PVT experiments (slimtube, rising-bubble, vanishing-IFT) cannot

Three-scenario phase-behaviour classification by p_min relative to p_sat and MMP_FC yields vaporisation-only, mixing-then-vaporisation, or mixing-only mechanisms; RF_C6+ vs RMI invariant to delta-p or cycle count

SRV multi-scale modelling workflow (rubble-fracture single-porosity at cm-m scale -> dual-porosity transfer term -> field/well DP+SP composite) with end-cycle recovery inversely proportional to rubble size
Dual-porosity vs single-porosity HnP gives strongly different produced GOR, API, C1, C7+ trends - those trends are themselves the diagnostic for which regime the field operates in

Numerical-dispersion sign-reversal: insufficient gridding gives artificially LOW recovery in conventional displacement but artificially HIGH recovery in HnP - reverses conventional grid-sensitivity intuition
Hammock plot (cumulative injected vs avg surface tubing pressure) exposes cycle-to-cycle hysteresis and confinement quality
builds_on: Carlsen et al. 2019 SPE-196056 fluid-sampling methodology (companion paper)
Conventional EOR-screening PVT experiments (slimtube, rising-bubble, vanishing-IFT)
EP Energy Eagle Ford pilot data
contributed_to: Operator HnP design and forecasting workflows in Eagle Ford, Bakken, Permian
Subsequent Whitson AS HnP and gas-EOR papers 2020-2022
Mydland-Whitson LRS series 2020-2021
Carlsen et al. 2021 SPE-205884 NRTA workflow
Whitson Academy HnP course material
collaborators: EP Energy team (E. Kerr, J. Nohavitza, M. Thuesen, J. H. Drozd, R. Ambrose)
summary_for_rag: Carlsen et al. 2019 URTeC-539 synthesises three perspectives on gas huff-n-puff (HnP) in the Eagle Ford: a novel CVI-CVD hybrid PVT experiment that captures the cyclic huff-puff structure, an SRV multi-scale simulation workflow (rubble-fracture -> dual-porosity transfer -> field composite), and a compositional-tracking methodology with two modes (sampling-available or production-only). Seven contributions including three-scenario phase-behaviour classification with RMI invariance, dual-porosity vs single-porosity composition-trend diagnostic, the counter-intuitive numerical-dispersion sign-reversal (insufficient gridding gives artificially HIGH recovery in HnP, opposite to conventional displacement), and the Hammock plot for confinement diagnostics.

title: Field-Wide Equation of State Model Development
journal: Unconventional Resources Technology Conference, Denver
key_findings: Field-wide (common) EOS concept: one EOS tuned simultaneously to 10s-100s of PVT samples covering all in-situ and produced fluids from discovery to abandonment; 35 years experience across 85+ fields
Field-wide gamma model with common alpha, eta, M_Li but sample-specific M_n+ - parsimonious yet able to track real compositional variation
alpha > 5 gamma shape as biodegradation / OBM-contamination diagnostic
Back-calculation of M_7+ and gamma_7+ from measured flashed-oil M_o and gamma_o (Eqs 3-4)
Composition variables as primary EOS tuning levers (recombination GOR +/-5%, gamma M_n+ +/-5-10%, C_N+ amount +/-25%) - novel practice; SPE Monograph 2000 uses component-property regression only
Ramping method for monotonic C7+ EOS parameter modification (single slope variable changes Tc/pc/omega progressively)
K-value monotonicity vs EOS-NBP as consistency validation (catches inconsistent BIP modifications)
Field-specific lower-bound Tb_Li(M_Li) correlation - novel field-wide NBP construction
Dynamic volume-shift update during C7+ property regression (preserves gamma_i(M_i))
Parent-child EOS workflow: tuned detailed field-wide EOS is parent; lumped EOS / BOPVT tables are derived children inheriting consistency
builds_on: 35 years of Whitson-group field/basin EOS-tuning practice across 85+ fields
contributed_to: Whitson AS commercial EOS-development service
Carlsen et al. 2019 SPE-196056 fluid-sampling paper (cites this paper as field-wide EOS underpinning)
Singh-Whitson-Nielsen 2025 SPE-225382 BOPVT vs lab-PVT comparison
Nielsen-Younus 2025 SPE-225470 Volve field end-to-end fluid model
Operator EOS-development workflows for Permian, Eagle Ford, Bakken, Montney, Middle East
Whitson Academy EOS development course material
Industry recognition contributing to 2023 AIME Honorary Membership for foremost technical achievement in petroleum fluid characterisation
summary_for_rag: Younus, Whitson, Alavian, Carlsen, Martinsen and Singh 2019 URTeC-551 distil 35 years of Whitson-group field-wide and basin-wide EOS-tuning experience across 85+ fields (Norway North Sea, Middle East giants, Australia, SE Asia, and unconventional basins Montney/Eagle Ford/Bakken/Permian/SCOOP-STACK). Ten methodology contributions including the field-wide gamma model with common alpha/eta/M_Li but sample-specific M_n+, composition variables as primary tuning levers (correcting an SPE Monograph 2000 blind spot), the ramping method for monotonic C7+ modification, K-value monotonicity vs EOS-NBP consistency validation, and the parent-child EOS workflow that lets every downstream application inherit the parent's consistency.

title: Compositional Tracking: Predicting Wellstream Compositions in Tight Unconventionals
journal: International Petroleum Technology Conference, Dhahran
key_findings: Three-tier compositional-tracking method hierarchy (Methods 1/2/3) keyed to data availability, sharing one field-wide EOS as common backbone

Method 3 novel direct-recombination wellstream prediction from measured separator-oil + separator-gas compositions and MW_o,C7+ with no regression - validated within line thickness across four fluid types
R_p-gamma_API curve smoothing as a Method-1.5 intermediate using a surface-process-normalised producing-GOR vs API relationship to remove stepwise-API artefacts between welltests
Seed-feed accuracy is fluid-type-specific - irrelevant for black oils, critical for near-critical fluids and gas condensates, correcting a nuance in the Hoda-Whitson 2013 insensitivity claim
Operational instruction: ask PVT labs to report measured MW_o,C7+ alongside separator-oil composition; without it Method 3 loses its regression-free property
builds_on: Hoda-Whitson 2013 SPE-164334 well-test rate conversion (Method 1 kernel)
Whitson-Sunjerga 2012 SPE-155499 LRS PVT framework (Method 2 kernel)
Fevang-Whitson 1994 ECM procedure for gas-condensate sampling
Younus et al. 2019 URTeC-551 Field-wide EOS (the common backbone)
contributed_to: Carlsen-Whitson 2020 IPTC-19775 separator SF/FF workflow
Carlsen-Dahouk-Hoffman-Whitson 2020 SPE-199982 CGR normalisation
Carlsen et al. 2021-2022 NRTA papers
Operator LRS workflows in Eagle Ford / Bakken / Permian / Montney / Vaca Muerta

Curtis's 2023 AIME Honorary Membership citation for influential consulting services

summary_for_rag: 2020 IPTC paper that consolidates Hoda-Whitson 2013, Whitson-Sunjerga 2012, and Carlsen-Whitson 2019 wellstream-prediction methods into a single three-tier hierarchy (Methods 1/2/3) keyed to data availability, and adds a novel no-regression Method 3 that recombines measured separator-oil and separator-gas compositions via material balance with no regression. Validated within line thickness across four fluid types (black oil to gas condensate). Introduces an R_p-gamma_API curve smoothing technique as a Method-1.5 intermediate. Corrects the Hoda-Whitson 2013 seed-feed-insensitivity claim with a fluid-type-dependent caveat. The compositional-tracking framework underpinning the Carlsen-Whitson NRTA series that followed.

title: Quantifying Separator Oil Shrinkage
journal: International Petroleum Technology Conference, Dhahran
key_findings: Shrinkage factor SF = 1/Bo,sep and flash factor FF = Rs,sep paired definition with exact total-GOR decomposition Rp = Rp,sep/SF + FF
Constant-SF practice STO-rate error: <2% black oil, ~5% volatile oil, ~10-15% gas condensate, up to ~20% near-critical volatile oil
Five red-flag screening criteria for material SF variation - Rsi >1500 scf/STB, rapid FBHP decline with shut-ins, separator-condition swings, low Tsep/high psep, gas-EOR/HnP
Three-step field-wide EOS daily workflow - tune EOS, predict daily wellstream via Methods 1/2/3, run through day-specific surface process
Four-equation no-EOS correlation fallback using rearranged Standing bubblepoint with closed-form FF after eliminating gas gravity via Katz 1942 correlation - three inputs (API, Tsep, psep)
builds_on: Carlsen-Dahouk-Mydland-Whitson 2020 IPTC-19596 compositional tracking (the daily wellstream-composition engine)
Younus et al. 2019 URTeC-551 field-wide EOS (the Step-1 EOS backbone)
Martinsen et al. 2010 SPE-135085 PVT framework
Whitson-Brule SPE Monograph 2000 Bo-Rs framework extended to surface train
contributed_to: Carlsen-Dahouk-Hoffman-Whitson 2020 SPE-199982 CGR normalisation
Carlsen-Whitson 2022 NRTA papers SPE-210420 / URTeC-3718584
Operator allocation, royalty, and reserves workflows in unconventional plays
Whitson Academy course material on separator handling
Operator daily-SCADA SF/FF calculation routines

collaborators.Mathias Lia Carlsen: Whitson AS senior engineer; first author of the 2020 IPTC pair (IPTC-19596 and IPTC-19775)

summary_for_rag: Companion paper to IPTC-19596. Develops the volumetric framework that converts metered separator-oil rates into stock-tank rates correctly in tight unconventionals where wellstream composition and separator conditions drift with time. Defines shrinkage factor SF and flash factor FF with exact total-GOR decomposition Rp = Rp,sep/SF + FF. Quantifies STO-rate error from constant-SF industry practice as fluid-type-specific (under 2% for black oils up to 20% for near-critical wells). Provides a three-step field-wide-EOS daily workflow and a four-equation no-EOS correlation fallback using rearranged Standing bubblepoint with closed-form FF.

title: CGR Normalization - Convert Rates to a Common Surface Process
journal: SPE Canada Unconventional Resources Conference, virtual
key_findings: Two-step CGR-normalization procedure - estimate flowing wellstream via four-tier method table, then re-process through a single common surface process to remove separator-condition-drift bias

summary_for_rag: Third paper in the 2020 Whitson AS surface-process triplet. Individual-fracture R_p(t) variance averages out at the commingled well header - well-level signal reproduces arithmetic-average-well, masking per-fracture variability

summary_for_rag: Methodology paper synthesising the two gas-based same-well cyclic EOR approaches relevant to tight unconventionals: Huff-n-Puff (HnP) and Fracture-to-Fracture (F2F). Establishes that HnP is mixing on the shattered volume governed by first-contact miscibility while F2F is displacement on the inter-fracture matrix governed by multi-contact miscibility. Names the MCM-irrelevant-for-HnP rule (zero-net-recovery-of-miscible-displacement from cyclic same-well architecture). Introduces the shattered volume plus small rubble plus molecular diffusion premise for HnP. Provides a pressure-dependent Tex,MOD multiplier to tune DPM HnP simulations to rock-scale fine-grid SPM, removing numerical-dispersion HnP-recovery inflation.

title: Compositional Tracking of a Huff-n-Puff Project in the Eagle Ford: A Second Take
journal: Unconventional Resources Technology Conference, Austin
key_findings: Wellstream C7+ signature is a direct HnP process-efficiency diagnostic - decreasing C7+ with BHP decline = efficient mixing (SV + diffusion); rising C7+ from injection-gas back to pre-implementation = inefficient piston-like displacement

Gas-condensate-area HnP wellstream C7+ 0.25-1 mol% vs black-oil-area 2 mol% - lower efficiency due to smaller EOR target volume not worse process; Boi<2 (Rsi<1000) wells have largest relative EOR target

Field validation of the SV/rubble hypothesis - C7+ signature matches DP simulation and contradicts SP planar-fracture; combined with Raterman 2017/2019 and Gale 2018 physical evidence, converts the shattered-volume premise to field-validated fact

Temperature-MMP inversion - MMP_MC proportional to T (worse conventional displacement); MMP_FC inversely proportional to T (better HnP mixing); higher-T reservoirs favour HnP but disfavour conventional miscible flooding
Three operational characteristics of successful gas HnP - large injection volumes, rapid large pressure buildup, modern completions creating large surface area via shattering

Two-part BOPVT table generation - lower table from CCE on initial fluid plus upper table via incremental swelling to a critical mixture; incremental-swelling extension preferred over single-stage swell and convergence-pressure negative-flash for stability
